name: Ilvaite Crystal — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Named for Ilva (ancient Elba), ilvaite occurs in contact metamorphic skarns. The black prismatic crystals from Serifos are among the finest in the world, associated with epidote an…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Named for Ilva (ancient Elba), ilvaite occurs in contact metamorphic skarns. The black prismatic crystals from Serifos are among the finest in the world, associated with epidote and calcite in combinations that show mineralogy's talent for chromatic contrast. The Greek island setting adds a mythological resonance.

This specimen originates from Serifos, Cyclades, Greece, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Indicolite Tourmaline — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Blue tourmaline — indicolite — requires iron in the right oxidation state during crystallization. Afghan specimens from the Kunar pegmatites rival anything from Brazil. The color r…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Blue tourmaline — indicolite — requires iron in the right oxidation state during crystallization. Afghan specimens from the Kunar pegmatites rival anything from Brazil. The color ranges from teal to deep ink-blue, with the finest showing a slightly greenish hue in one direction (tourmaline is strongly pleochroic).

This specimen originates from Paprok, Nuristan, Afghanistan, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Wollastonite Spray

description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Wollastonite forms in contact metamorphic zones where limestone meets igneous intrusions — limestone calcium reacting with silica-rich fluids at high temperature. name: Benitoite Crystal on Natrolite — Miniature
short_description: California's state gemstone is found in one commercial location on Earth: the Benitoite Gem Mine in San Benito County. The deep sapphire-blue crystals sit on snow-white natrolite m…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. California's state gemstone is found in one commercial location on Earth: the Benitoite Gem Mine in San Benito County. The deep sapphire-blue crystals sit on snow-white natrolite matrices in a combination so striking it feels staged. Under short-wave UV they fluoresce an electric blue that beggers description. Active collecting ended; existing specimens are a finite resource.

This specimen originates from San Benito County, California, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Vanadinite on Barite — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Vanadinite's intense red-orange hexagonal prisms on white barite matrix are one of mineralogy's most photogenic combinations. This Moroccan locality is the world standard for the s…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Vanadinite's intense red-orange hexagonal prisms on white barite matrix are one of mineralogy's most photogenic combinations. This Moroccan locality is the world standard for the species. The color comes from vanadium — the same element used to strengthen steel — here precipitated from oxidizing lead ore deposits.

This specimen originates from Mibladen, Khénifra, Morocco, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Topaz — Imperial Orange
short_description: Imperial topaz in the precious sherry-to-deep-orange range is found in one place on Earth: the hills above Ouro Preto. The color comes from chromophoric impurities stabilized durin…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Imperial topaz in the precious sherry-to-deep-orange range is found in one place on Earth: the hills above Ouro Preto. The color comes from chromophoric impurities stabilized during crystallization in topaz-bearing pegmatites. Brazilian law once restricted export to protect royal reserves. Some specimens still carry that weight of exclusivity.

This specimen originates from Ouro Preto, Minas Gerais, Brazil, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Dioptase Crystal Cluster — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: When dioptase was first brought to Europe in 1785, it was initially mistaken for emerald — the color is that intense. Namibia's Tsumeb mine is the world's greatest dioptase source,…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. When dioptase was first brought to Europe in 1785, it was initially mistaken for emerald — the color is that intense. Namibia's Tsumeb mine is the world's greatest dioptase source, producing emerald-green crystals on white calcite that remain unsurpassed. The copper-silicate structure produces a color no other mineral quite replicates.

This specimen originates from Tsumeb, Namibia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Green Fluorite Cubic Cluster — Display Piece
short_description: Hunan province produces prolific fluorite in every color, but the emerald-green cubic crystals from Xianghualing — sometimes meter-sized — are a category apart. The green comes fro…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Hunan province produces prolific fluorite in every color, but the emerald-green cubic crystals from Xianghualing — sometimes meter-sized — are a category apart. The green comes from trace yttrium and other rare earth elements. Under short-wave UV, they fluoresce intensely blue — fluorite gave fluorescence its name.

This specimen originates from Xianghualing Mine, Hunan, China, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Euclase Crystal — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Euclase — named for its perfect cleavage (Greek: 'eu' + 'klasis') — is a beryllium-aluminum silicate of extraordinary rarity. Colombian material from emerald-producing regions show…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Euclase — named for its perfect cleavage (Greek: 'eu' + 'klasis') — is a beryllium-aluminum silicate of extraordinary rarity. Colombian material from emerald-producing regions shows pale blue to blue-green color. Because of its perfect cleavage it rarely survives gemcutting intact, making crystals more valuable than faceted stones.

The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Stibnite's needle-like crystals, silver-black and mirror-bright, cluster in formations that look like abstract metallic sculptures — or, in the imagination of Japanese sword-makers who once prized it, the quintessence of a blade. Hunan's Xikuangshan district is the world's largest antimony producer and its specimens are legendary.

name: Rhodochrosite Stalactite Slice — Specimen Grade
short_description: Argentina's Capillitas mine produces stalactitic rhodochrosite — pink manganese carbonate deposited in layers by ancient hydrothermal solutions. Sliced and polished, the cross-sect…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Argentina's Capillitas mine produces stalactitic rhodochrosite — pink manganese carbonate deposited in layers by ancient hydrothermal solutions. Sliced and polished, the cross-section reveals concentric rose-and-cream banding that looks like a geological dessert. The Incas called rhodochrosite 'Inca Rose.'

description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Pyrrhotite — iron sulfide with a magnetic personality — forms bronze-coloured hexagonal plates at Dalnegorsk, often accompanied by sphalerite and galena. It is slightly magnetic due to a non-stoichiometric iron lattice, which means some specimens will move a compass needle. A mineral with an invisible force.

name: Pyrite Cube Cluster — Miniature
short_description: Navajún produces the most geometrically perfect pyrite cubes on Earth — faces so flat they reflect like mirrors, edges so sharp they appear machined. The formation mechanism is deb…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Navajún produces the most geometrically perfect pyrite cubes on Earth — faces so flat they reflect like mirrors, edges so sharp they appear machined. The formation mechanism is debated but involves replacement of organic material in marine sediments. Each cube grew slowly in Cretaceous seafloor mud, achieving precision a factory would envy.

This specimen originates from Navajún, La Rioja, Spain, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Iceland Spar — Optical Calcite — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Iceland spar — transparent, colorless calcite — famously shows double refraction: place a crystal over text and see two images. It was used in Viking navigation (the 'sunstone' of …
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Iceland spar — transparent, colorless calcite — famously shows double refraction: place a crystal over text and see two images. It was used in Viking navigation (the 'sunstone' of sagas) and later in optical instruments before glass could be made pure enough. The Helgustaðir deposit in Iceland is the historical source.

description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Euclase — named for its perfect cleavage (Greek: 'eu' + 'klasis') — is a beryllium-aluminum silicate of extraordinary rarity. Colombian material from emerald-producing regions shows pale blue to blue-green color. Because of its perfect cleavage it rarely survives gemcutting intact, making crystals more valuable than faceted stones.

short_description: Realgar's scarlet-orange crystals on yellow orpiment creates a composition that Renaissance painters literally ground up for pigment — 'King's Yellow' and 'Realgar Red.' Both are a…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Realgar's scarlet-orange crystals on yellow orpiment creates a composition that Renaissance painters literally ground up for pigment — 'King's Yellow' and 'Realgar Red.' Both are arsenic sulfides, so these specimens are handled with care and not displayed in sunlight. Orpiment converts to arsenolite in UV; the colors fade. Keep them dark.

name: Selenite Gypsum Wand — Miniature
short_description: The Cave of Crystals under Naica contains selenite beams up to 12 meters long — the largest natural crystals on Earth. These collector-size wands from the same deposit are crystall…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The Cave of Crystals under Naica contains selenite beams up to 12 meters long — the largest natural crystals on Earth. These collector-size wands from the same deposit are crystallographically identical: pure, colorless, with perfect cleavage and a translucency that turns light creamy white. They grew over 500,000 years in 50°C saline groundwater.

There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Elba produces hematite in a spectacular form: 'iron roses' — tabular crystals arranged in rosette clusters that look, unmistakably, like metallic flowers. description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Vanadinite's intense red-orange hexagonal prisms on white barite matrix are one of mineralogy's most photogenic combinations. This Moroccan locality is the world standard for the species. The color comes from vanadium — the same element used to strengthen steel — here precipitated from oxidizing lead ore deposits.

The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Illinois' Cave-in-Rock fluorite district produced some of history's greatest fluorite specimens — giant cubic and octahedral crystals in purple, yellow, and green. description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Muzo Colombian emeralds are a geological anomaly — formed not in pegmatites like most beryls, but in black shales rich in carbon, chromium, and vanadium. The resulting color is the purest green in the mineral kingdom. Owning a Muzo emerald crystal in its original calcite matrix is owning a piece of the world's most romanticized mine.

name: Native Copper Arborescent
short_description: Michigan's Keweenaw Peninsula contains the world's largest deposit of native copper — pure copper metal, not ore, occurring in basalt amygdales and conglomerate beds. It was mined …
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Michigan's Keweenaw Peninsula contains the world's largest deposit of native copper — pure copper metal, not ore, occurring in basalt amygdales and conglomerate beds. It was mined by Indigenous peoples for thousands of years before European contact. Arborescent ('tree-like') specimens show copper's natural tendency to branch as it grows from solution.

The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Hunan province produces prolific fluorite in every color, but the emerald-green cubic crystals from Xianghualing — sometimes meter-sized — are a category apart. Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Navajún produces the most geometrically perfect pyrite cubes on Earth — faces so flat they reflect like mirrors, edges so sharp they appear machined. description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Zircon is Earth's oldest mineral — crystals from Western Australia have been dated at 4.4 billion years, older than any rock formation. Sri Lankan zircons are typically 540–600 million years old and are prized for their adamantine luster and dispersion that rivals diamond. They contain the earliest clock in Earth's geological record.

This specimen originates from Ratnapura District, Sri Lanka, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Botswana agate forms in volcanic amygdales — gas bubbles in ancient lava flows — over millions of years as chalcedony layers deposit concentrically. The fortification pattern maps each episode of deposition: a geological autobiography in concentric arcs.

short_description: Kunzite — pink spodumene — was first described from Pala, California in 1902 and named after mineralogist George Kunz, Tiffany's legendary gem buyer. The lilac-pink to rose color c…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Kunzite — pink spodumene — was first described from Pala, California in 1902 and named after mineralogist George Kunz, Tiffany's legendary gem buyer. The lilac-pink to rose color comes from trace manganese. Kunzite is strongly pleochroic: rotate it and the color shifts from vivid pink to near-colorless. It is also sensitive to heat and prolonged light exposure — a delicate beauty.

name: Malachite Botryoidal Specimen
short_description: The peacock-green banding of malachite records episodes of copper-rich groundwater flowing through limestone — each ring a chapter of chemistry. Katanga's Tenke district is the wor…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. The peacock-green banding of malachite records episodes of copper-rich groundwater flowing through limestone — each ring a chapter of chemistry. Katanga's Tenke district is the world's most productive copper basin, and its malachite is correspondingly spectacular: deep greens, tight banding, botryoidal surfaces like compacted velvet.

This specimen originates from Tenke Mine, Katanga, DR Congo, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Alexandrite Crystal — Color-Change — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: The original alexandrite — discovered in the Ural Mountains on Tsar Alexander II's birthday in 1834 — changes from emerald green in daylight to raspberry red in incandescent light.…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. The original alexandrite — discovered in the Ural Mountains on Tsar Alexander II's birthday in 1834 — changes from emerald green in daylight to raspberry red in incandescent light. This is chromium absorbing specific wavelengths differently under different light sources. Uralian material is rarer than diamonds; a fine crystal is one of mineralogy's supreme achievements.

This specimen originates from Malysheva, Ural Mountains, Russia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Native Silver Wires — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Kongsberg Silver Mine, active from 1623 to 1957, produced native silver in forms unmatched anywhere: wire silver twisted into delicate knots, sheet silver, and arborescent silver '…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Kongsberg Silver Mine, active from 1623 to 1957, produced native silver in forms unmatched anywhere: wire silver twisted into delicate knots, sheet silver, and arborescent silver 'trees' weighing hundreds of kilograms. A Kongsberg wire silver specimen is a piece of Scandinavian mining history — and one of the most photogenic objects in mineralogy.

name: Tanzanite Crystal — Unheated
short_description: Tanzanite — blue-purple zoisite — was discovered in 1967, reportedly by a Maasai herder who noticed blue stones scattered by a lightning-caused brush fire. The heat had converted b…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Tanzanite — blue-purple zoisite — was discovered in 1967, reportedly by a Maasai herder who noticed blue stones scattered by a lightning-caused brush fire. The heat had converted brown trichroic zoisite into the blue-purple we prize. An unheated natural crystal shows the raw trichroism: blue, purple, and burgundy shifting with viewing direction.

description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Labradorescence — that ghostly play of blue, green, and gold — occurs when light diffracts between ultra-thin feldspar layers deposited during slow magmatic cooling. Each specimen is a prism masquerading as stone. Turn it in sunlight and it remembers something ancient.

name: Calcite Dog-Tooth Cluster — Display Piece
short_description: Tennessee's Elmwood zinc mine produced calcite specimens of museum quality — scalenohedral 'dogtooth' crystals several centimeters long, water-clear, often with galena or sphalerit…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Tennessee's Elmwood zinc mine produced calcite specimens of museum quality — scalenohedral 'dogtooth' crystals several centimeters long, water-clear, often with galena or sphalerite partners. The mine closed but these specimens endure as benchmarks. Calcite cleaves in three directions and shows strong double refraction.

This specimen originates from Elmwood Mine, Tennessee, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. collector_notes: Dust with a soft brush; avoid water for soft minerals (hardness below 4).

name: Pyrrhotite Crystal — Display Piece
short_description: Pyrrhotite — iron sulfide with a magnetic personality — forms bronze-coloured hexagonal plates at Dalnegorsk, often accompanied by sphalerite and galena. It is slightly magnetic du…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Pyrrhotite — iron sulfide with a magnetic personality — forms bronze-coloured hexagonal plates at Dalnegorsk, often accompanied by sphalerite and galena. It is slightly magnetic due to a non-stoichiometric iron lattice, which means some specimens will move a compass needle. A mineral with an invisible force.

Large gem-quality crystals are essentially uncollectable; even small bright specimens are significant finds.

short_description: Chalcopyrite — brassy yellow, iridescent when tarnished — is Earth's primary copper ore, but Peru's Huaron mine produces crystals of sculptural quality atop contrasting dark sphale…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Chalcopyrite — brassy yellow, iridescent when tarnished — is Earth's primary copper ore, but Peru's Huaron mine produces crystals of sculptural quality atop contrasting dark sphalerite matrices. The iridescence comes from thin oxide layers that act as diffraction gratings, creating peacock-like color play.

short_description: California's state gemstone is found in one commercial location on Earth: the Benitoite Gem Mine in San Benito County. The deep sapphire-blue crystals sit on snow-white natrolite m…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. California's state gemstone is found in one commercial location on Earth: the Benitoite Gem Mine in San Benito County. The deep sapphire-blue crystals sit on snow-white natrolite matrices in a combination so striking it feels staged. Under short-wave UV they fluoresce an electric blue that beggers description. Active collecting ended; existing specimens are a finite resource.

description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Imperial topaz in the precious sherry-to-deep-orange range is found in one place on Earth: the hills above Ouro Preto. The color comes from chromophoric impurities stabilized during crystallization in topaz-bearing pegmatites. Brazilian law once restricted export to protect royal reserves. Some specimens still carry that weight of exclusivity.

name: Zircon Crystal in Pegmatite — Miniature
short_description: Zircon is Earth's oldest mineral — crystals from Western Australia have been dated at 4.4 billion years, older than any rock formation. Sri Lankan zircons are typically 540–600 mil…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Zircon is Earth's oldest mineral — crystals from Western Australia have been dated at 4.4 billion years, older than any rock formation. Sri Lankan zircons are typically 540–600 million years old and are prized for their adamantine luster and dispersion that rivals diamond. They contain the earliest clock in Earth's geological record.

This specimen originates from Ratnapura District, Sri Lanka, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Chalcopyrite Crystal on Sphalerite — Miniature

description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Chalcopyrite — brassy yellow, iridescent when tarnished — is Earth's primary copper ore, but Peru's Huaron mine produces crystals of sculptural quality atop contrasting dark sphalerite matrices. The iridescence comes from thin oxide layers that act as diffraction gratings, creating peacock-like color play.

This specimen originates from Huaron, Pasco, Peru, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Zircon Crystal in Pegmatite — Display Piece
short_description: Zircon is Earth's oldest mineral — crystals from Western Australia have been dated at 4.4 billion years, older than any rock formation. Sri Lankan zircons are typically 540–600 mil…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Zircon is Earth's oldest mineral — crystals from Western Australia have been dated at 4.4 billion years, older than any rock formation. Sri Lankan zircons are typically 540–600 million years old and are prized for their adamantine luster and dispersion that rivals diamond. They contain the earliest clock in Earth's geological record.

description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. The floating blue glow — adularescence — is light scattering off alternating layers of orthoclase and albite feldspar. description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Manganite — black, striated, and metallic — forms in low-temperature hydrothermal veins and is the primary ore of manganese. The Ilfeld locality in Germany's Harz Mountains produced reference-quality prismatic crystals in the 19th century. These specimens helped define the species; some remain in museum collections that are the envy of science.

description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. California's state gemstone is found in one commercial location on Earth: the Benitoite Gem Mine in San Benito County. The deep sapphire-blue crystals sit on snow-white natrolite matrices in a combination so striking it feels staged. Under short-wave UV they fluoresce an electric blue that beggers description. description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Green spodumene — hiddenite — is arguably the rarest gem-quality mineral found in the United States. Named for W.E. Hidden who first found it in North Carolina in 1879, its emerald-green color comes from chromium and vanadium. Large gem-quality crystals are essentially uncollectable; even small bright specimens are significant finds.

Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Discovered in 1967 along the Kenya-Tanzania border by British geologist Campbell Bridges, tsavorite quickly rivaled emerald for intense green color — with greater hardness, better clarity, and no oiling treatments. name: Staurolite Twin — Fairy Stone
short_description: Staurolite crystals famously twin at 60° or 90° angles, creating natural crosses. Virginia fairy stones were worn as charms long before Europeans arrived — Cherokee legend held the…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Staurolite crystals famously twin at 60° or 90° angles, creating natural crosses. Virginia fairy stones were worn as charms long before Europeans arrived — Cherokee legend held they formed from the tears of forest fairies mourning a tragedy. The 'tears crystallized as they hit the ground' is, geologically, not that far from the truth of how contact metamorphism works.

short_description: Red beryl — bixbite — forms in a single deposit: Utah's Wah Wah Mountains, where rhyolitic volcanism created exactly the right chemical conditions for manganese-bearing beryl. It i…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Red beryl — bixbite — forms in a single deposit: Utah's Wah Wah Mountains, where rhyolitic volcanism created exactly the right chemical conditions for manganese-bearing beryl. It is estimated to be 1,000 times rarer than emerald. Gem-quality crystals over 2 carats are nearly nonexistent. A small, clean crystal is a mineralogical unicorn.

description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. For decades, painite held the record as Earth's rarest mineral — only two specimens were known to science until 2001. Mogok's alluvial deposits then yielded a few hundred crystals, but fine gem-quality pieces remain extraordinary. The orange-red to brownish-red color comes from iron and chromium. This is mineralogy's equivalent of finding a new species of large mammal.

Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Sillimanite is one of three aluminum silicate polymorphs (kyanite and andalusite being the others), each stable at different temperature-pressure conditions. description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Kongsberg Silver Mine, active from 1623 to 1957, produced native silver in forms unmatched anywhere: wire silver twisted into delicate knots, sheet silver, and arborescent silver 'trees' weighing hundreds of kilograms. A Kongsberg wire silver specimen is a piece of Scandinavian mining history — and one of the most photogenic objects in mineralogy.

name: Ruby Crystal in Matrix
short_description: Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lat…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lattice, combined with strong fluorescence that makes Mogok stones glow under sunlight. These crystals grew in marble limestone under conditions so specific that the Mogok Stone Tract remains the world's premier ruby source after centuries of mining.

name: Burmese Amber with Lizard Inclusion
short_description: Burmese amber at 99 million years old contains the most spectacular fossil record of Cretaceous forest life — including the first feathered dinosaur tail in amber, frogs, lizards, …
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Burmese amber at 99 million years old contains the most spectacular fossil record of Cretaceous forest life — including the first feathered dinosaur tail in amber, frogs, lizards, and intact flowers. A lizard inclusion of this age puts you within 35 million years of T. rex. These specimens are actively studied; owning one is participating in ongoing paleontology.

This specimen originates from Hukawng Valley, Kachin State, Myanmar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.
formation: This specimen formed through geological processes operating over timescales that dwarf human history. The specific combination of chemistry, temperature, pressure, and time at this locality produced material of exceptional quality that makes it a valued addition to any serious collection.

name: Star Sapphire — 6-Ray — Gem Rough
short_description: The asterism in a star sapphire is caused by needle-like inclusions of rutile — titanium dioxide — arranged in three intersecting sets, following the hexagonal symmetry of corundum…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The asterism in a star sapphire is caused by needle-like inclusions of rutile — titanium dioxide — arranged in three intersecting sets, following the hexagonal symmetry of corundum. When cut as a cabochon, these needles diffract light into a six-rayed star that moves as you tilt the stone. Mogok produces the finest: velvety blue with a sharp, centered star.

This specimen originates from Mogok, Myanmar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Spinel Crystal — Red
short_description: Some of history's most famous 'rubies' — the Black Prince's Ruby in the British Crown Jewels, the Timur Ruby — are actually spinels. Spinel and ruby occur together in Mogok and are…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Some of history's most famous 'rubies' — the Black Prince's Ruby in the British Crown Jewels, the Timur Ruby — are actually spinels. Spinel and ruby occur together in Mogok and are nearly identical in color. Spinel is now rightly valued in its own right: it comes in vivid reds, oranges, pinks, blues, and purples, with exceptional brilliance.

name: Color-Change Garnet — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Madagascar's color-change garnets perform a trick more dramatic than alexandrite: green to blue-green in daylight, red to purple in incandescent light. The mechanism is chromium ab…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Madagascar's color-change garnets perform a trick more dramatic than alexandrite: green to blue-green in daylight, red to purple in incandescent light. The mechanism is chromium absorption, identical to alexandrite's, but in a garnet matrix that intensifies the saturation. This discovery (1990s) upended the gem market's assumptions about garnet's color range.

name: Cat's Eye Chrysoberyl
short_description: The chatoyancy in cat's eye chrysoberyl — a single bright line that moves with light, exactly replicating a feline iris — comes from parallel channels or inclusions of actinolite a…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. The chatoyancy in cat's eye chrysoberyl — a single bright line that moves with light, exactly replicating a feline iris — comes from parallel channels or inclusions of actinolite aligned during crystal growth. In the gem trade, 'cat's eye' alone always means chrysoberyl; other cat's eyes require a qualifying mineral name. Sri Lanka's production is the world benchmark.

name: Diamond Crystal — Rough Octahedron
short_description: A diamond octahedron is carbon atoms arranged in the most efficient three-dimensional lattice possible. This geometry emerged over a billion years ago, 150 kilometers underground, …
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. A diamond octahedron is carbon atoms arranged in the most efficient three-dimensional lattice possible. This geometry emerged over a billion years ago, 150 kilometers underground, under pressures that would crush a submarine. It was then blasted to the surface in a kimberlite eruption. The hardest natural substance on Earth began as something ordinary: carbon.

name: Star Ruby — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: A six-rayed star on red corundum — the finest rubies showing asterism are among the rarest gems. Vietnam's Luc Yen district produces rubies with exceptional color, often a pure pig…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. A six-rayed star on red corundum — the finest rubies showing asterism are among the rarest gems. Vietnam's Luc Yen district produces rubies with exceptional color, often a pure pigeon's blood without the pink or orange tones that afflict Thai or Indian material. The star is caused by rutile silk oriented by the trigonal symmetry of corundum.

short_description: The most prized andradite garnet has the highest dispersion of any natural gem — more 'fire' than diamond. The legendary Russian Ural deposits are mostly exhausted; Italian Val Mal…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. The most prized andradite garnet has the highest dispersion of any natural gem — more 'fire' than diamond. The legendary Russian Ural deposits are mostly exhausted; Italian Val Malenco now produces the finest material. The 'horsetail' inclusion — chrysotile asbestos fibers radiating from a chromite crystal — is considered a positive identifier of Russian origin and paradoxically increases value.

description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Whitby jet — compressed fossil wood from 180-million-year-old monkey puzzle trees — became famous during Queen Victoria's mourning period. Victorian jewelers carved it into elaborate pieces; Whitby's jet workshops were legendary. The deep black, lightweight warmth, and workability of jet make it unique among organic gems. A carved piece is Victorian history in your hand.

name: Padparadscha Sapphire Crystal
short_description: The most sought-after sapphire color has no proper English name. 'Padparadscha' is Sinhalese for lotus blossom — that particular blend of pink and orange that sits at the exact int…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. The most sought-after sapphire color has no proper English name. 'Padparadscha' is Sinhalese for lotus blossom — that particular blend of pink and orange that sits at the exact intersection of sunrise and flower. The color comes from trace iron and chromium. Madagascar's Ilakaka deposits yield fine material, though the definition of 'true' padparadscha remains contentious among gemmologists.

locality: Batalha, Rio Grande do Norte, Brazil
short_description: In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalin…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalines emerged — copper-bearing, with a color so vivid it appeared internally lit. Nothing like it had been seen. A Paraíba tourmaline of fine quality per carat now rivals the finest emeralds and rubies.

name: Ruby Crystal in Matrix — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lat…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lattice, combined with strong fluorescence that makes Mogok stones glow under sunlight. These crystals grew in marble limestone under conditions so specific that the Mogok Stone Tract remains the world's premier ruby source after centuries of mining.

name: Spinel Crystal — Cobalt Blue — Gem Rough
short_description: Cobalt-blue spinel is the rarest color variety — the vivid blue comes from cobalt substituting for magnesium. Vietnam's Luc Yen produces specimens of electrifying blue, small but i…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Cobalt-blue spinel is the rarest color variety — the vivid blue comes from cobalt substituting for magnesium. Vietnam's Luc Yen produces specimens of electrifying blue, small but intense. The color is essentially the same mechanism as cobalt glass: cobalt's electronic structure absorbs red and green wavelengths, reflecting only blue.

This specimen originates from Luc Yen, Vietnam, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Tsavorite Garnet Crystal — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Tsavorite's deep green rivals emerald but with greater hardness and clarity — it rarely needs treatment, unlike most emeralds. The color comes from vanadium and chromium in grossul…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Tsavorite's deep green rivals emerald but with greater hardness and clarity — it rarely needs treatment, unlike most emeralds. The color comes from vanadium and chromium in grossular garnet. Found only in the East African metamorphic belt, new deposits are sought eagerly by miners on both sides of the Kenya-Tanzania border. Fine crystals in matrix are rarer than the faceted gems.

short_description: Baltic amber — Eocene tree resin, 44 million years old — is history's most productive source of fossil inclusions. When an insect, spider, or plant fragment became trapped, it coul…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Baltic amber — Eocene tree resin, 44 million years old — is history's most productive source of fossil inclusions. When an insect, spider, or plant fragment became trapped, it could be preserved in atomic detail longer than any other fossilization mode. This specimen contains a complete insect visible under a loupe. Jurassic Park was not entirely fiction.

name: Grandidierite Crystal — Collector Grade
short_description: Grandidierite was described in 1902 from Madagascar and for decades was represented in mineral collections by a handful of specimens from that original locality. Its teal-blue colo…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Grandidierite was described in 1902 from Madagascar and for decades was represented in mineral collections by a handful of specimens from that original locality. Its teal-blue color comes from iron; it is strongly trichroic, showing blue-green, colorless, and dark blue-green from different directions. Facetable quality remained elusive until a new Malagasy find in 2014.

This specimen originates from Tranomaro, Anosy Region, Madagascar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Titanite (sphene) has a dispersion exceeding diamond — more 'fire' than any other common transparent gem — combined with a yellow-green color that shifts with viewing angle. name: Demantoid Garnet — Horsetail Inclusion — Gem Rough
short_description: The most prized andradite garnet has the highest dispersion of any natural gem — more 'fire' than diamond. The legendary Russian Ural deposits are mostly exhausted; Italian Val Mal…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. The most prized andradite garnet has the highest dispersion of any natural gem — more 'fire' than diamond. The legendary Russian Ural deposits are mostly exhausted; Italian Val Malenco now produces the finest material. The 'horsetail' inclusion — chrysotile asbestos fibers radiating from a chromite crystal — is considered a positive identifier of Russian origin and paradoxically increases value.

name: Fire Opal Crystal — Gem Rough
short_description: Mexican fire opal is the only opal prized primarily for its body color rather than play-of-color — an intense orange-red that no other gem replicates. It forms in volcanic rhyolite…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Mexican fire opal is the only opal prized primarily for its body color rather than play-of-color — an intense orange-red that no other gem replicates. It forms in volcanic rhyolites, where silica-rich solutions cool and deposit opal in gas cavities. Transparent fire opal can be faceted; the resulting gems look like drops of flame.

locality: Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia
short_description: Lightning Ridge black opal is the most valuable gem variety on a per-carat basis that the Earth produces routinely. The 'black' refers to the dark body tone, which provides the bac…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Lightning Ridge black opal is the most valuable gem variety on a per-carat basis that the Earth produces routinely. The 'black' refers to the dark body tone, which provides the backdrop against which play-of-color — caused by diffraction from stacked silica spheres of uniform size — blazes most intensely. Each opal's pattern is unique, a frozen snapshot of spheres arranged by sedimentation 100 million years ago.

name: Rough Emerald Crystal — Gem Grade
short_description: The Coscuez mine, one of three great Colombian emerald mines alongside Muzo and Chivor, produces a distinctive bright green. Colombian emeralds form in black shale and calcite vein…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The Coscuez mine, one of three great Colombian emerald mines alongside Muzo and Chivor, produces a distinctive bright green. Colombian emeralds form in black shale and calcite veins — a hydrothermal system unique on Earth. A gem-grade rough crystal still in matrix represents the stone before the cutter's art intervenes: the raw material of history's most coveted colored gem.

This specimen originates from Coscuez Mine, Boyacá, Colombia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Padparadscha Sapphire Crystal — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: The most sought-after sapphire color has no proper English name. 'Padparadscha' is Sinhalese for lotus blossom — that particular blend of pink and orange that sits at the exact int…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. The most sought-after sapphire color has no proper English name. 'Padparadscha' is Sinhalese for lotus blossom — that particular blend of pink and orange that sits at the exact intersection of sunrise and flower. The color comes from trace iron and chromium. Madagascar's Ilakaka deposits yield fine material, though the definition of 'true' padparadscha remains contentious among gemmologists.

name: Cat's Eye Chrysoberyl — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: The chatoyancy in cat's eye chrysoberyl — a single bright line that moves with light, exactly replicating a feline iris — comes from parallel channels or inclusions of actinolite a…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. The chatoyancy in cat's eye chrysoberyl — a single bright line that moves with light, exactly replicating a feline iris — comes from parallel channels or inclusions of actinolite aligned during crystal growth. In the gem trade, 'cat's eye' alone always means chrysoberyl; other cat's eyes require a qualifying mineral name. Sri Lanka's production is the world benchmark.

Vietnam's Luc Yen district produces rubies with exceptional color, often a pure pigeon's blood without the pink or orange tones that afflict Thai or Indian material. The star is caused by rutile silk oriented by the trigonal symmetry of corundum.

name: Ruby Crystal in Matrix — Natural Crystal
short_description: Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lat…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lattice, combined with strong fluorescence that makes Mogok stones glow under sunlight. These crystals grew in marble limestone under conditions so specific that the Mogok Stone Tract remains the world's premier ruby source after centuries of mining.

This specimen originates from Mogok Valley, Mandalay Region, Myanmar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Kornerupine is a collector's gem — rarely seen in jewelry, spectacular in a collection. The brown-green color changes to reddish in incandescent light. Mogok produces the finest material. The mineral was named after Danish geologist Andreas Nikolaus Kornerup in 1884. Fine facetable crystals are genuinely rare.

description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Sri Lankan sapphires have been prized since the Silk Road era — their distinctive cornflower blue, softer and more luminous than Kashmir or Montana material, made Ceylon the default source for royal jewelry for centuries. The color comes from iron and titanium impurities in corundum. A natural, unheated crystal of this quality is rarer than the faceted gem.

Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Cobalt-blue spinel is the rarest color variety — the vivid blue comes from cobalt substituting for magnesium. Vietnam's Luc Yen produces specimens of electrifying blue, small but intense. The color is essentially the same mechanism as cobalt glass: cobalt's electronic structure absorbs red and green wavelengths, reflecting only blue.

name: Coral Specimen — Red
short_description: Noble coral — Corallium rubrum — is the red skeleton of a colonial marine animal, deposited as microcrystalline calcite. Mediterranean red coral has been harvested since antiquity;…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Noble coral — Corallium rubrum — is the red skeleton of a colonial marine animal, deposited as microcrystalline calcite. Mediterranean red coral has been harvested since antiquity; Phoenicians traded it along ancient routes. Today it is heavily regulated; this specimen is from a documented pre-regulation collection. name: Star Sapphire — 6-Ray — Natural Crystal
short_description: The asterism in a star sapphire is caused by needle-like inclusions of rutile — titanium dioxide — arranged in three intersecting sets, following the hexagonal symmetry of corundum…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. The asterism in a star sapphire is caused by needle-like inclusions of rutile — titanium dioxide — arranged in three intersecting sets, following the hexagonal symmetry of corundum. When cut as a cabochon, these needles diffract light into a six-rayed star that moves as you tilt the stone. Mogok produces the finest: velvety blue with a sharp, centered star.

description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Baltic amber — Eocene tree resin, 44 million years old — is history's most productive source of fossil inclusions. When an insect, spider, or plant fragment became trapped, it could be preserved in atomic detail longer than any other fossilization mode. This specimen contains a complete insect visible under a loupe. Jurassic Park was not entirely fiction.

name: Paraíba Tourmaline — Neon Blue — Large Example
locality: Batalha, Rio Grande do Norte, Brazil
short_description: In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalin…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalines emerged — copper-bearing, with a color so vivid it appeared internally lit. Nothing like it had been seen. A Paraíba tourmaline of fine quality per carat now rivals the finest emeralds and rubies.

description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. The most sought-after sapphire color has no proper English name. 'Padparadscha' is Sinhalese for lotus blossom — that particular blend of pink and orange that sits at the exact intersection of sunrise and flower. The color comes from trace iron and chromium. Madagascar's Ilakaka deposits yield fine material, though the definition of 'true' padparadscha remains contentious among gemmologists.

Titanite (sphene) has a dispersion exceeding diamond — more 'fire' than any other common transparent gem — combined with a yellow-green color that shifts with viewing angle. description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Some of history's most famous 'rubies' — the Black Prince's Ruby in the British Crown Jewels, the Timur Ruby — are actually spinels. Spinel and ruby occur together in Mogok and are nearly identical in color. Spinel is now rightly valued in its own right: it comes in vivid reds, oranges, pinks, blues, and purples, with exceptional brilliance.

description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Harlequin pattern — large, mosaic-like patches of color in a geometric arrangement — is the rarest and most valued opal pattern. Coober Pedy, discovered in 1915 by a teenage boy on a water prospecting trip, remains the world's largest opal mining area. The town is largely underground, residents having extended their mines into habitable homes to escape the 50°C surface temperatures.

name: Cat's Eye Chrysoberyl — Large Example
short_description: The chatoyancy in cat's eye chrysoberyl — a single bright line that moves with light, exactly replicating a feline iris — comes from parallel channels or inclusions of actinolite a…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. The chatoyancy in cat's eye chrysoberyl — a single bright line that moves with light, exactly replicating a feline iris — comes from parallel channels or inclusions of actinolite aligned during crystal growth. In the gem trade, 'cat's eye' alone always means chrysoberyl; other cat's eyes require a qualifying mineral name. Sri Lanka's production is the world benchmark.

This specimen originates from Ratnapura, Sri Lanka, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Grandidierite was described in 1902 from Madagascar and for decades was represented in mineral collections by a handful of specimens from that original locality. description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. For most of the 20th century, jeremejevite was represented in world collections by fewer than a dozen crystals from Siberia. Then Namibian material emerged — pale blue crystals of exceptional clarity. Named after Russian mineralogist Pavel Jeremejev, it produces a pale aquamarine to colorless gem. Its rarity puts it alongside painite in conversations about the most inaccessible gems.

description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Natural pink topaz is extraordinarily rare — most pink topaz on the market is heat-treated or irradiated colorless topaz. Pakistan's Katlang produces genuine pink crystals where chromium substitutes for aluminum. The color can be pastel pink or deep rose, and it is stable under light and heat, unlike many artificially colored gems.

description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. A diamond octahedron is carbon atoms arranged in the most efficient three-dimensional lattice possible. This geometry emerged over a billion years ago, 150 kilometers underground, under pressures that would crush a submarine. It was then blasted to the surface in a kimberlite eruption. The hardest natural substance on Earth began as something ordinary: carbon.

name: Ruby Crystal in Matrix — Gem Rough
short_description: Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lat…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lattice, combined with strong fluorescence that makes Mogok stones glow under sunlight. These crystals grew in marble limestone under conditions so specific that the Mogok Stone Tract remains the world's premier ruby source after centuries of mining.

description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Whitby jet — compressed fossil wood from 180-million-year-old monkey puzzle trees — became famous during Queen Victoria's mourning period. Victorian jewelers carved it into elaborate pieces; Whitby's jet workshops were legendary. The deep black, lightweight warmth, and workability of jet make it unique among organic gems. A carved piece is Victorian history in your hand.

description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Some of history's most famous 'rubies' — the Black Prince's Ruby in the British Crown Jewels, the Timur Ruby — are actually spinels. Spinel and ruby occur together in Mogok and are nearly identical in color. Spinel is now rightly valued in its own right: it comes in vivid reds, oranges, pinks, blues, and purples, with exceptional brilliance.

name: Sapphire Crystal — Cornflower Blue
short_description: Sri Lankan sapphires have been prized since the Silk Road era — their distinctive cornflower blue, softer and more luminous than Kashmir or Montana material, made Ceylon the defaul…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Sri Lankan sapphires have been prized since the Silk Road era — their distinctive cornflower blue, softer and more luminous than Kashmir or Montana material, made Ceylon the default source for royal jewelry for centuries. The color comes from iron and titanium impurities in corundum. A natural, unheated crystal of this quality is rarer than the faceted gem.

This specimen originates from Ratnapura, Sabaragamuwa Province, Sri Lanka, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Color-Change Garnet — Natural Crystal
short_description: Madagascar's color-change garnets perform a trick more dramatic than alexandrite: green to blue-green in daylight, red to purple in incandescent light. The mechanism is chromium ab…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Madagascar's color-change garnets perform a trick more dramatic than alexandrite: green to blue-green in daylight, red to purple in incandescent light. The mechanism is chromium absorption, identical to alexandrite's, but in a garnet matrix that intensifies the saturation. This discovery (1990s) upended the gem market's assumptions about garnet's color range.

This specimen originates from Bekily, Anosy Region, Madagascar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Tsavorite Garnet Crystal
short_description: Tsavorite's deep green rivals emerald but with greater hardness and clarity — it rarely needs treatment, unlike most emeralds. The color comes from vanadium and chromium in grossul…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Tsavorite's deep green rivals emerald but with greater hardness and clarity — it rarely needs treatment, unlike most emeralds. The color comes from vanadium and chromium in grossular garnet. Found only in the East African metamorphic belt, new deposits are sought eagerly by miners on both sides of the Kenya-Tanzania border. Fine crystals in matrix are rarer than the faceted gems.

short_description: Kornerupine is a collector's gem — rarely seen in jewelry, spectacular in a collection. The brown-green color changes to reddish in incandescent light. Mogok produces the finest ma…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Kornerupine is a collector's gem — rarely seen in jewelry, spectacular in a collection. The brown-green color changes to reddish in incandescent light. Mogok produces the finest material. The mineral was named after Danish geologist Andreas Nikolaus Kornerup in 1884. Fine facetable crystals are genuinely rare.

name: White Opal — Harlequin — Natural Crystal
short_description: Harlequin pattern — large, mosaic-like patches of color in a geometric arrangement — is the rarest and most valued opal pattern. Coober Pedy, discovered in 1915 by a teenage boy on…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Harlequin pattern — large, mosaic-like patches of color in a geometric arrangement — is the rarest and most valued opal pattern. Coober Pedy, discovered in 1915 by a teenage boy on a water prospecting trip, remains the world's largest opal mining area. The town is largely underground, residents having extended their mines into habitable homes to escape the 50°C surface temperatures.

This specimen originates from Coober Pedy, South Australia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Star Sapphire — 6-Ray — Collector Grade
short_description: The asterism in a star sapphire is caused by needle-like inclusions of rutile — titanium dioxide — arranged in three intersecting sets, following the hexagonal symmetry of corundum…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. The asterism in a star sapphire is caused by needle-like inclusions of rutile — titanium dioxide — arranged in three intersecting sets, following the hexagonal symmetry of corundum. When cut as a cabochon, these needles diffract light into a six-rayed star that moves as you tilt the stone. Mogok produces the finest: velvety blue with a sharp, centered star.

This specimen originates from Mogok, Myanmar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Burmese amber at 99 million years old contains the most spectacular fossil record of Cretaceous forest life — including the first feathered dinosaur tail in amber, frogs, lizards, and intact flowers. A lizard inclusion of this age puts you within 35 million years of T. rex. These specimens are actively studied; owning one is participating in ongoing paleontology.

name: Paraíba Tourmaline — Neon Blue
locality: Batalha, Rio Grande do Norte, Brazil
short_description: In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalin…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalines emerged — copper-bearing, with a color so vivid it appeared internally lit. Nothing like it had been seen. A Paraíba tourmaline of fine quality per carat now rivals the finest emeralds and rubies.

'Padparadscha' is Sinhalese for lotus blossom — that particular blend of pink and orange that sits at the exact intersection of sunrise and flower. The color comes from trace iron and chromium. Madagascar's Ilakaka deposits yield fine material, though the definition of 'true' padparadscha remains contentious among gemmologists.

This specimen originates from Ilakaka, Ihorombe Region, Madagascar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.

name: Tsavorite Garnet Crystal — Gem Rough

description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Tsavorite's deep green rivals emerald but with greater hardness and clarity — it rarely needs treatment, unlike most emeralds. The color comes from vanadium and chromium in grossular garnet. Found only in the East African metamorphic belt, new deposits are sought eagerly by miners on both sides of the Kenya-Tanzania border. Fine crystals in matrix are rarer than the faceted gems.

name: Cat's Eye Chrysoberyl — Exceptional Quality
short_description: The chatoyancy in cat's eye chrysoberyl — a single bright line that moves with light, exactly replicating a feline iris — comes from parallel channels or inclusions of actinolite a…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. The chatoyancy in cat's eye chrysoberyl — a single bright line that moves with light, exactly replicating a feline iris — comes from parallel channels or inclusions of actinolite aligned during crystal growth. In the gem trade, 'cat's eye' alone always means chrysoberyl; other cat's eyes require a qualifying mineral name. Sri Lanka's production is the world benchmark.

name: Diamond Crystal — Rough Octahedron — Gem Rough
short_description: A diamond octahedron is carbon atoms arranged in the most efficient three-dimensional lattice possible. This geometry emerged over a billion years ago, 150 kilometers underground, …
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. A diamond octahedron is carbon atoms arranged in the most efficient three-dimensional lattice possible. This geometry emerged over a billion years ago, 150 kilometers underground, under pressures that would crush a submarine. It was then blasted to the surface in a kimberlite eruption. The hardest natural substance on Earth began as something ordinary: carbon.

This specimen originates from Jwaneng Mine, Botswana, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Demantoid Garnet — Horsetail Inclusion — Gem Rough
short_description: The most prized andradite garnet has the highest dispersion of any natural gem — more 'fire' than diamond. The legendary Russian Ural deposits are mostly exhausted; Italian Val Mal…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. The most prized andradite garnet has the highest dispersion of any natural gem — more 'fire' than diamond. The legendary Russian Ural deposits are mostly exhausted; Italian Val Malenco now produces the finest material. The 'horsetail' inclusion — chrysotile asbestos fibers radiating from a chromite crystal — is considered a positive identifier of Russian origin and paradoxically increases value.

name: Ruby Crystal in Matrix — Gem Rough
short_description: Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lat…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Mogok rubies are the world's standard — the 'pigeon's blood' color that all other rubies are compared against. This comes from chromium replacing aluminum in corundum's crystal lattice, combined with strong fluorescence that makes Mogok stones glow under sunlight. These crystals grew in marble limestone under conditions so specific that the Mogok Stone Tract remains the world's premier ruby source after centuries of mining.

description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Titanite (sphene) has a dispersion exceeding diamond — more 'fire' than any other common transparent gem — combined with a yellow-green color that shifts with viewing angle. Swiss Alpine specimens are the world benchmark: sharp wedge-shaped crystals on calcite matrices from classic localities. The name sphene comes from the Greek for 'wedge.'

name: Burmese Amber with Lizard Inclusion — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Burmese amber at 99 million years old contains the most spectacular fossil record of Cretaceous forest life — including the first feathered dinosaur tail in amber, frogs, lizards, …
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Burmese amber at 99 million years old contains the most spectacular fossil record of Cretaceous forest life — including the first feathered dinosaur tail in amber, frogs, lizards, and intact flowers. A lizard inclusion of this age puts you within 35 million years of T. rex. These specimens are actively studied; owning one is participating in ongoing paleontology.

name: Jet Carved Specimen — Collector Grade
short_description: Whitby jet — compressed fossil wood from 180-million-year-old monkey puzzle trees — became famous during Queen Victoria's mourning period. Victorian jewelers carved it into elabora…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Whitby jet — compressed fossil wood from 180-million-year-old monkey puzzle trees — became famous during Queen Victoria's mourning period. Victorian jewelers carved it into elaborate pieces; Whitby's jet workshops were legendary. The deep black, lightweight warmth, and workability of jet make it unique among organic gems. A carved piece is Victorian history in your hand.

short_description: The Coscuez mine, one of three great Colombian emerald mines alongside Muzo and Chivor, produces a distinctive bright green. Colombian emeralds form in black shale and calcite vein…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The Coscuez mine, one of three great Colombian emerald mines alongside Muzo and Chivor, produces a distinctive bright green. Colombian emeralds form in black shale and calcite veins — a hydrothermal system unique on Earth. A gem-grade rough crystal still in matrix represents the stone before the cutter's art intervenes: the raw material of history's most coveted colored gem.

name: Paraíba Tourmaline — Neon Blue — Natural Crystal
locality: Batalha, Rio Grande do Norte, Brazil
short_description: In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalin…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalines emerged — copper-bearing, with a color so vivid it appeared internally lit. Nothing like it had been seen. A Paraíba tourmaline of fine quality per carat now rivals the finest emeralds and rubies.

Vietnam's Luc Yen district produces rubies with exceptional color, often a pure pigeon's blood without the pink or orange tones that afflict Thai or Indian material. The star is caused by rutile silk oriented by the trigonal symmetry of corundum.

name: Spinel Crystal — Cobalt Blue — Exceptional Quality
short_description: Cobalt-blue spinel is the rarest color variety — the vivid blue comes from cobalt substituting for magnesium. Vietnam's Luc Yen produces specimens of electrifying blue, small but i…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Cobalt-blue spinel is the rarest color variety — the vivid blue comes from cobalt substituting for magnesium. Vietnam's Luc Yen produces specimens of electrifying blue, small but intense. The color is essentially the same mechanism as cobalt glass: cobalt's electronic structure absorbs red and green wavelengths, reflecting only blue.

This specimen originates from Luc Yen, Vietnam, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Color-Change Garnet — Collector Grade
short_description: Madagascar's color-change garnets perform a trick more dramatic than alexandrite: green to blue-green in daylight, red to purple in incandescent light. The mechanism is chromium ab…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Madagascar's color-change garnets perform a trick more dramatic than alexandrite: green to blue-green in daylight, red to purple in incandescent light. The mechanism is chromium absorption, identical to alexandrite's, but in a garnet matrix that intensifies the saturation. This discovery (1990s) upended the gem market's assumptions about garnet's color range.

name: Sapphire Crystal — Cornflower Blue — Natural Crystal
short_description: Sri Lankan sapphires have been prized since the Silk Road era — their distinctive cornflower blue, softer and more luminous than Kashmir or Montana material, made Ceylon the defaul…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Sri Lankan sapphires have been prized since the Silk Road era — their distinctive cornflower blue, softer and more luminous than Kashmir or Montana material, made Ceylon the default source for royal jewelry for centuries. The color comes from iron and titanium impurities in corundum. A natural, unheated crystal of this quality is rarer than the faceted gem.

name: Black Opal — Collector Grade
locality: Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia
short_description: Lightning Ridge black opal is the most valuable gem variety on a per-carat basis that the Earth produces routinely. The 'black' refers to the dark body tone, which provides the bac…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Lightning Ridge black opal is the most valuable gem variety on a per-carat basis that the Earth produces routinely. The 'black' refers to the dark body tone, which provides the backdrop against which play-of-color — caused by diffraction from stacked silica spheres of uniform size — blazes most intensely. Each opal's pattern is unique, a frozen snapshot of spheres arranged by sedimentation 100 million years ago.

description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Grandidierite was described in 1902 from Madagascar and for decades was represented in mineral collections by a handful of specimens from that original locality. Its teal-blue color comes from iron; it is strongly trichroic, showing blue-green, colorless, and dark blue-green from different directions. Facetable quality remained elusive until a new Malagasy find in 2014.

Coober Pedy, discovered in 1915 by a teenage boy on a water prospecting trip, remains the world's largest opal mining area. name: Paraíba Tourmaline — Neon Blue — Gem Rough

short_description: In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalin…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. In 1987, Heitor Dimas Barbosa started digging a hillside in Paraíba state, Brazil, convinced something extraordinary lay within. He was right. In 1989 the first neon-blue tourmalines emerged — copper-bearing, with a color so vivid it appeared internally lit. Nothing like it had been seen. A Paraíba tourmaline of fine quality per carat now rivals the finest emeralds and rubies.

This specimen originates from Batalha, Rio Grande do Norte, Brazil, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Black Opal — Natural Crystal
locality: Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia
short_description: Lightning Ridge black opal is the most valuable gem variety on a per-carat basis that the Earth produces routinely. The 'black' refers to the dark body tone, which provides the bac…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Lightning Ridge black opal is the most valuable gem variety on a per-carat basis that the Earth produces routinely. The 'black' refers to the dark body tone, which provides the backdrop against which play-of-color — caused by diffraction from stacked silica spheres of uniform size — blazes most intensely. Each opal's pattern is unique, a frozen snapshot of spheres arranged by sedimentation 100 million years ago.

This specimen originates from Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Natural pink topaz is extraordinarily rare — most pink topaz on the market is heat-treated or irradiated colorless topaz. Pakistan's Katlang produces genuine pink crystals where chromium substitutes for aluminum. The color can be pastel pink or deep rose, and it is stable under light and heat, unlike many artificially colored gems.

name: Jet Carved Specimen — Exceptional Quality
short_description: Whitby jet — compressed fossil wood from 180-million-year-old monkey puzzle trees — became famous during Queen Victoria's mourning period. Victorian jewelers carved it into elabora…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Whitby jet — compressed fossil wood from 180-million-year-old monkey puzzle trees — became famous during Queen Victoria's mourning period. Victorian jewelers carved it into elaborate pieces; Whitby's jet workshops were legendary. The deep black, lightweight warmth, and workability of jet make it unique among organic gems. A carved piece is Victorian history in your hand.

This specimen originates from Whitby, North Yorkshire, England, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Star Ruby
short_description: A six-rayed star on red corundum — the finest rubies showing asterism are among the rarest gems. Vietnam's Luc Yen district produces rubies with exceptional color, often a pure pig…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. A six-rayed star on red corundum — the finest rubies showing asterism are among the rarest gems. Vietnam's Luc Yen district produces rubies with exceptional color, often a pure pigeon's blood without the pink or orange tones that afflict Thai or Indian material. The star is caused by rutile silk oriented by the trigonal symmetry of corundum.

name: Coral Specimen — Red — Cabinet Specimen
short_description: Noble coral — Corallium rubrum — is the red skeleton of a colonial marine animal, deposited as microcrystalline calcite. Mediterranean red coral has been harvested since antiquity;…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Noble coral — Corallium rubrum — is the red skeleton of a colonial marine animal, deposited as microcrystalline calcite. Mediterranean red coral has been harvested since antiquity; Phoenicians traded it along ancient routes. Today it is heavily regulated; this specimen is from a documented pre-regulation collection. The color ranges from pale pink to 'oxblood' red, the deepest shades most prized.

This specimen originates from Sciacca, Sicily, Mediterranean Sea, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Titanite (sphene) has a dispersion exceeding diamond — more 'fire' than any other common transparent gem — combined with a yellow-green color that shifts with viewing angle. description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Baltic amber — Eocene tree resin, 44 million years old — is history's most productive source of fossil inclusions. This specimen contains a complete insect visible under a loupe. Jurassic Park was not entirely fiction.

description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. A diamond octahedron is carbon atoms arranged in the most efficient three-dimensional lattice possible. This geometry emerged over a billion years ago, 150 kilometers underground, under pressures that would crush a submarine. It was then blasted to the surface in a kimberlite eruption. The hardest natural substance on Earth began as something ordinary: carbon.
