name: Fish Fossil — Green River Formation — Small Specimen
short_description: The Green River Formation — Eocene lake beds in Wyoming, Colorado, and Utah — is one of Earth's great fossil deposits. Fish are preserved in such fine-grained varve (annual) layers…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. The Green River Formation — Eocene lake beds in Wyoming, Colorado, and Utah — is one of Earth's great fossil deposits. Fish are preserved in such fine-grained varve (annual) layers that individual scales are visible under a loupe, and the stomach contents of some specimens can be identified. These Eocene fish died approximately 50 million years ago, on a calm day in a subtropical lake.

This specimen originates from Kemmerer, Lincoln County, Wyoming, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.
formation: Fossilization is a rare event: fewer than 1 in 10,000 organisms ever become fossils, and even fewer survive to be collected. This specimen represents a successful preservation — chemistry, burial, and geological luck combining to maintain structural detail across millions of years.
locality_detail: Fish Fossil — Green River Formation — Small Specimen from Kemmerer, Lincoln County, Wyoming, USA. This locality is known for producing specimens of exceptional quality.
collector_notes: Store on a padded surface; avoid contact with harder minerals.

name: Insect in Amber — Ant Cluster — Prepared Specimen
short_description: Social insects — ants, bees, termites — evolved their complex societies in the Eocene or earlier, and Baltic amber preserves them in behavioral poses: workers, queens, males, and s…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Social insects — ants, bees, termites — evolved their complex societies in the Eocene or earlier, and Baltic amber preserves them in behavioral poses: workers, queens, males, and sometimes entire colonies trapped together. An ant preserved in amber carries all the social information of its species frozen 44 million years ago. Evolution runs on deep time.

This specimen originates from Baltic Region, Poland/Russia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Ammonite — Polished Section — Small Specimen
short_description: Cut and polished, an ammonite's internal chambers reveal the logarithmic spiral that mathematicians called the 'golden ratio' centuries before they understood its name. The iridesc…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Cut and polished, an ammonite's internal chambers reveal the logarithmic spiral that mathematicians called the 'golden ratio' centuries before they understood its name. The iridescent nacreous layer — preserved aragonite from the original shell — creates colors that shift with viewing angle. These Cretaceous cephalopods died 66 million years ago; their shells are more beautiful than they could have imagined.

This specimen originates from Mahajanga Province, Madagascar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Dinosaur Tooth — Carcharodontosaurus — Prepared Specimen
short_description: Carcharodontosaurus — 'shark-toothed lizard' — was one of the largest theropod dinosaurs, rivaling T. rex in body size. Its blade-like serrated teeth, up to 8 centimeters long, cou…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Carcharodontosaurus — 'shark-toothed lizard' — was one of the largest theropod dinosaurs, rivaling T. rex in body size. Its blade-like serrated teeth, up to 8 centimeters long, could slice through flesh with a lateral shaking motion. This specimen from Morocco's Late Cretaceous Kem Kem Formation is 95 million years old.

name: Nautiloid — Cyrtoceras Orthocerid — Field-Collected
short_description: Straight-shelled nautiloids — orthocerids — were among the apex predators of Ordovician and Silurian seas, reaching several meters in length. These Moroccan specimens, 380–420 mill…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Straight-shelled nautiloids — orthocerids — were among the apex predators of Ordovician and Silurian seas, reaching several meters in length. These Moroccan specimens, 380–420 million years old, are polished to reveal the internal septa (dividing walls) that the living animal used for buoyancy control, flooding chambers with gas or water as needed. Living nautiluses still use this mechanism.

This specimen originates from Erfoud, Morocco, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Ammonite — Hildoceras bifrons — Small Specimen
short_description: Whitby's Jurassic shales have yielded ammonites since the Anglo-Saxon era — local legend turned them into petrified snakes (they lacked the heads, so locals carved them on). The py…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Whitby's Jurassic shales have yielded ammonites since the Anglo-Saxon era — local legend turned them into petrified snakes (they lacked the heads, so locals carved them on). The pyritized Hildoceras bifrons specimens, 180 million years old, show the ribbed sutures that distinguish each ammonite genus. Yorkshire's coast is actively eroding, releasing new specimens each winter storm.

This specimen originates from Whitby, North Yorkshire, England, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Coprolite — Predator Specimen — Museum Replica
short_description: Coprolites are fossils of feces, and they are paleontology's most underappreciated resource. Analysis of what was consumed (bone fragments, scales, feathers, plant material) tells …
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Coprolites are fossils of feces, and they are paleontology's most underappreciated resource. Analysis of what was consumed (bone fragments, scales, feathers, plant material) tells us more about ancient diets than bones alone. Montana's Judith River Formation (Cretaceous) yields coprolites from large theropods, identifiable by crushed bone content, size, and context.

This specimen originates from Judith River Formation, Montana, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Plesiosaurian Vertebra — Small Specimen
short_description: The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian vertebra is diagnostic: the distinctive hourglass shape, with a central channel for the notochord, identifies an animal whose lineage includes the long-necked forms that inspired the Loch Ness myth.

This specimen originates from Kimmeridge Clay, Dorset, England, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Dinosaur Tooth — Spinosaurus — Display Grade
short_description: Spinosaurus aegyptiacus — 95–100 million years old — may have been the largest predatory dinosaur that ever lived, longer than T. rex and adapted for catching fish with conical, un…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Spinosaurus aegyptiacus — 95–100 million years old — may have been the largest predatory dinosaur that ever lived, longer than T. rex and adapted for catching fish with conical, unserrated teeth more like a crocodile's. This tooth from Morocco's Kem Kem Formation is from a specimen of that predator, recovered from sediments that were once a vast river delta.

This specimen originates from Kem Kem Formation, Morocco, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Trilobite — Walliserops trifurcatus (Trident)
short_description: Walliserops trifurcatus is palaeontology's most improbable arthropod: a trilobite with a long, ornate trident growing from its head. The function of this structure remains debated …
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Walliserops trifurcatus is palaeontology's most improbable arthropod: a trilobite with a long, ornate trident growing from its head. The function of this structure remains debated — competition display? Prey manipulation? — but its geometric elaboration suggests evolution's willingness to experiment. Only a few hundred specimens are known. Each is a unanswered question in stone.

This specimen originates from Foum Zguid, Morocco, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Spider in Amber — Burma — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: Burmese amber's 99-million-year age places its inclusions squarely in the Cretaceous — contemporary with apex predator dinosaurs. Spiders from this period belong to lineages that l…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Burmese amber's 99-million-year age places its inclusions squarely in the Cretaceous — contemporary with apex predator dinosaurs. Spiders from this period belong to lineages that look remarkably similar to modern relatives, a testament to the effectiveness of the spider body plan. Some Burmese amber spiders have been found in silk-wrapped prey — hunting behavior frozen at the moment of success.

This specimen originates from Hukawng Valley, Myanmar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Mammoth Molar — Prepared Specimen
short_description: The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Plei…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Pleistocene megafauna remains up to 1.8 million years old. A mammoth molar's ridged enamel plates, evolved for grinding tough grasses on frigid steppes, is still recognizable: evolution's answer to permafrost diets.

This specimen originates from North Sea Beds, Netherlands, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Plant Fossil — Sigillaria — Prepared Specimen
short_description: Sigillaria was a coal-swamp tree reaching 30 meters, with a bark pattern of hexagonal leaf scars that looks like a reptile's scales. The Carboniferous forests of 310 million years …
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Sigillaria was a coal-swamp tree reaching 30 meters, with a bark pattern of hexagonal leaf scars that looks like a reptile's scales. The Carboniferous forests of 310 million years ago were composed of such trees alongside giant horsetails and ferns — all now extinct genera. Their compressed remains are literally the coal seams that powered the industrial revolution.

This specimen originates from Carboniferous, Saarland, Germany, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Petrified Wood — Araucarioxylon — Prepared Specimen
short_description: 228-million-year-old trees from the Triassic floodplains of what would become Arizona were buried in volcanic ash, silicified over millions of years, then exposed by erosion. The o…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. 228-million-year-old trees from the Triassic floodplains of what would become Arizona were buried in volcanic ash, silicified over millions of years, then exposed by erosion. The original wood cells are replaced by quartz or chalcedony in precise detail — annual rings, cellular structure, and even fungal damage preserved in stone. These trees predated both mammals and birds.

This specimen originates from Petrified Forest, Arizona, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Trilobite — Enrolled Phacops
short_description: Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual c…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual calcite lenses each oriented to minimize spherical aberration, represent an optical system that evolution independently re-discovered in modern compound eyes. The Moroccan Sahara was once a shallow tropical sea.

This specimen originates from Hamar Laghdad, Erfoud, Morocco, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Mosasaur Tooth on Matrix
short_description: Mosasaurs ruled Cretaceous seas for 23 million years — massive predatory lizards (not dinosaurs) up to 17 meters long, with double-hinged jaws that could swallow large prey whole. …
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Mosasaurs ruled Cretaceous seas for 23 million years — massive predatory lizards (not dinosaurs) up to 17 meters long, with double-hinged jaws that could swallow large prey whole. This tooth, still embedded in jaw matrix, is from the latest Cretaceous (~70 million years ago). These animals and the non-avian dinosaurs went extinct simultaneously in the end-Cretaceous mass extinction.

This specimen originates from Khouribga Province, Morocco, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Trace Fossil — Dinosaur Footprint Cast — Prepared Specimen
short_description: The Connecticut Valley contains one of North America's richest trace fossil records — Triassic dinosaurs walking across mudflats that dried and lithified. Trace fossils record beha…
description:

The Connecticut Valley contains one of North America's richest trace fossil records — Triassic dinosaurs walking across mudflats that dried and lithified. Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Carcharodontosaurus — 'shark-toothed lizard' — was one of the largest theropod dinosaurs, rivaling T. rex in body size. name: Spider in Amber — Burma — Display Grade
short_description: Burmese amber's 99-million-year age places its inclusions squarely in the Cretaceous — contemporary with apex predator dinosaurs. Spiders from this period belong to lineages that l…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Burmese amber's 99-million-year age places its inclusions squarely in the Cretaceous — contemporary with apex predator dinosaurs. Spiders from this period belong to lineages that look remarkably similar to modern relatives, a testament to the effectiveness of the spider body plan. Some Burmese amber spiders have been found in silk-wrapped prey — hunting behavior frozen at the moment of success.

name: Megalodon Tooth Replica — Detailed
short_description: Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking in ocean trenches, as popularized fiction suggests). These precise casts of actual 7-centimeter teeth from South Carolina show the serrations clearly. The original sharks are gone; the geometry of their teeth endures.

name: Shark Teeth — Odontaspis Cluster — Small Specimen

short_description: Lee Creek's Miocene (5–20 million years ago) phosphate deposits are among North America's richest fossil marine vertebrate sites. The sand tiger shark Odontaspis produced multi-roo…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Lee Creek's Miocene (5–20 million years ago) phosphate deposits are among North America's richest fossil marine vertebrate sites. The sand tiger shark Odontaspis produced multi-rooted, needle-like teeth that formed in conveyor-belt succession — sharks shed and replace thousands of teeth in a lifetime. A cluster plate shows this biological abundance fossilized.

name: Trilobite — Enrolled Phacops — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual c…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual calcite lenses each oriented to minimize spherical aberration, represent an optical system that evolution independently re-discovered in modern compound eyes. The Moroccan Sahara was once a shallow tropical sea.

short_description: Whitby's Jurassic shales have yielded ammonites since the Anglo-Saxon era — local legend turned them into petrified snakes (they lacked the heads, so locals carved them on). The py…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Whitby's Jurassic shales have yielded ammonites since the Anglo-Saxon era — local legend turned them into petrified snakes (they lacked the heads, so locals carved them on). The pyritized Hildoceras bifrons specimens, 180 million years old, show the ribbed sutures that distinguish each ammonite genus. Yorkshire's coast is actively eroding, releasing new specimens each winter storm.

name: Plesiosaurian Vertebra — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian vertebra is diagnostic: the distinctive hourglass shape, with a central channel for the notochord, identifies an animal whose lineage includes the long-necked forms that inspired the Loch Ness myth.

name: Petrified Wood — Araucarioxylon — Display Grade
short_description: 228-million-year-old trees from the Triassic floodplains of what would become Arizona were buried in volcanic ash, silicified over millions of years, then exposed by erosion. The o…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. 228-million-year-old trees from the Triassic floodplains of what would become Arizona were buried in volcanic ash, silicified over millions of years, then exposed by erosion. The original wood cells are replaced by quartz or chalcedony in precise detail — annual rings, cellular structure, and even fungal damage preserved in stone. These trees predated both mammals and birds.

name: Mammoth Molar — Small Specimen
short_description: The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Plei…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Pleistocene megafauna remains up to 1.8 million years old. A mammoth molar's ridged enamel plates, evolved for grinding tough grasses on frigid steppes, is still recognizable: evolution's answer to permafrost diets.

name: Nautiloid — Cyrtoceras Orthocerid
short_description: Straight-shelled nautiloids — orthocerids — were among the apex predators of Ordovician and Silurian seas, reaching several meters in length. These Moroccan specimens, 380–420 mill…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Straight-shelled nautiloids — orthocerids — were among the apex predators of Ordovician and Silurian seas, reaching several meters in length. These Moroccan specimens, 380–420 million years old, are polished to reveal the internal septa (dividing walls) that the living animal used for buoyancy control, flooding chambers with gas or water as needed. Living nautiluses still use this mechanism.

name: Brachiopod Cluster — Spirifer — Small Specimen
short_description: Brachiopods look superficially like clams but are unrelated — a classic example of convergent evolution. They dominated seafloors for 300 million years. Devonian Spirifer brachiopo…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Brachiopods look superficially like clams but are unrelated — a classic example of convergent evolution. They dominated seafloors for 300 million years. Devonian Spirifer brachiopods from New York state show the distinctive 'wings' and delicate ribbing of a filter-feeding animal that lived in dense communities on warm, shallow seafloors. The Devonian mass extinction largely ended their dominance.

name: Sea Urchin — Micraster — Field-Collected
short_description: The white chalk of southern England — the White Cliffs of Dover — was deposited between 70–100 million years ago as a thick ooze of coccolithophore shells (single-celled algae) on …
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The white chalk of southern England — the White Cliffs of Dover — was deposited between 70–100 million years ago as a thick ooze of coccolithophore shells (single-celled algae) on a warm, shallow sea floor. Within it, heart urchins like Micraster were buried and preserved in three dimensions, their test intact. British farmers once called them 'fairy loaves' and placed them on hearths for luck.

short_description: Eurypterids — 'sea scorpions' — were the apex predators of Silurian seas and freshwaters, some reaching 2.5 meters. This specimen, approximately 430 million years old, preserves th…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Eurypterids — 'sea scorpions' — were the apex predators of Silurian seas and freshwaters, some reaching 2.5 meters. This specimen, approximately 430 million years old, preserves the articulated appendages and paddle-like swimming legs of an arthropod lineage that ruled for 200 million years before dying in the Permian extinction. Their nearest living relatives are horseshoe crabs.

These Cretaceous cephalopods died 66 million years ago; their shells are more beautiful than they could have imagined.

name: Glyptodon Osteoderms — Small Specimen
short_description: Glyptodon — an armadillo the size of a Volkswagen Beetle — roamed South America until approximately 10,000 years ago. Its rigid shell was composed of thousands of interlocking oste…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Glyptodon — an armadillo the size of a Volkswagen Beetle — roamed South America until approximately 10,000 years ago. Its rigid shell was composed of thousands of interlocking osteoderms (bone plates) hexagonal in pattern, like the shell of a turtle but evolved independently in armadillo relatives. Early humans likely hunted glyptodons; some shells show evidence of having been used as shelters.

description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Spinosaurus aegyptiacus — 95–100 million years old — may have been the largest predatory dinosaur that ever lived, longer than T. rex and adapted for catching fish with conical, unserrated teeth more like a crocodile's. This tooth from Morocco's Kem Kem Formation is from a specimen of that predator, recovered from sediments that were once a vast river delta.

Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Mazon Creek concretions — iron-carbonate nodules from Carboniferous coal measures — have yielded some of the most diverse fossil flora on Earth. Crack one open and find a perfect fern frond, preserved in three dimensions, from a coal swamp 309 million years ago. This specimen predates the dinosaurs by 75 million years. Carboniferous forests built the coal we burned for the industrial revolution.

This specimen originates from Mazon Creek Formation, Illinois, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Trace Fossil — Dinosaur Footprint Cast — Field-Collected
short_description: The Connecticut Valley contains one of North America's richest trace fossil records — Triassic dinosaurs walking across mudflats that dried and lithified. Trace fossils record beha…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. The Connecticut Valley contains one of North America's richest trace fossil records — Triassic dinosaurs walking across mudflats that dried and lithified. Trace fossils record behavior, not bodies: the weight distribution of an animal in mid-stride, its pace length, whether it was running. This three-toed track was made 200 million years ago by an early theropod whose skeleton has never been found.

short_description: The horseshoe crab lineage has changed so little in 450 million years that Limulus today is nearly identical to Silurian relatives. This Eocene specimen from the Green River Format…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The horseshoe crab lineage has changed so little in 450 million years that Limulus today is nearly identical to Silurian relatives. This Eocene specimen from the Green River Formation (~50 million years old) is a near-perfect preservation of a 'living fossil' mid-career — the animal looked essentially like this long before humans existed, and still does. Time is a patient sculptor.

short_description: Cut and polished, an ammonite's internal chambers reveal the logarithmic spiral that mathematicians called the 'golden ratio' centuries before they understood its name. The iridesc…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Cut and polished, an ammonite's internal chambers reveal the logarithmic spiral that mathematicians called the 'golden ratio' centuries before they understood its name. The iridescent nacreous layer — preserved aragonite from the original shell — creates colors that shift with viewing angle. These Cretaceous cephalopods died 66 million years ago; their shells are more beautiful than they could have imagined.

name: Sea Urchin — Micraster — Museum Replica
short_description: The white chalk of southern England — the White Cliffs of Dover — was deposited between 70–100 million years ago as a thick ooze of coccolithophore shells (single-celled algae) on …
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. The white chalk of southern England — the White Cliffs of Dover — was deposited between 70–100 million years ago as a thick ooze of coccolithophore shells (single-celled algae) on a warm, shallow sea floor. Within it, heart urchins like Micraster were buried and preserved in three dimensions, their test intact. British farmers once called them 'fairy loaves' and placed them on hearths for luck.

short_description: The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian vertebra is diagnostic: the distinctive hourglass shape, with a central channel for the notochord, identifies an animal whose lineage includes the long-necked forms that inspired the Loch Ness myth.

name: Megalodon Tooth Replica — Detailed — Display Grade
short_description: Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking in ocean trenches, as popularized fiction suggests). These precise casts of actual 7-centimeter teeth from South Carolina show the serrations clearly. The original sharks are gone; the geometry of their teeth endures.

This specimen originates from South Carolina, USA (cast from original), one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Crinoid Stem Section — Polished — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: Crinoids — 'sea lilies' — are filter-feeding echinoderms that have existed for 480 million years. Their multi-jointed stems, when polished in cross-section, reveal a perfectly five…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Crinoids — 'sea lilies' — are filter-feeding echinoderms that have existed for 480 million years. Their multi-jointed stems, when polished in cross-section, reveal a perfectly five-fold symmetric pattern that looks like a star or flower. Entire Mississippian limestone beds are composed of little else: a living carpet of these animals covered Paleozoic sea floors to horizon.

short_description: Burmese amber's 99-million-year age places its inclusions squarely in the Cretaceous — contemporary with apex predator dinosaurs. Spiders from this period belong to lineages that l…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Burmese amber's 99-million-year age places its inclusions squarely in the Cretaceous — contemporary with apex predator dinosaurs. Spiders from this period belong to lineages that look remarkably similar to modern relatives, a testament to the effectiveness of the spider body plan. Some Burmese amber spiders have been found in silk-wrapped prey — hunting behavior frozen at the moment of success.

short_description: Eurypterids — 'sea scorpions' — were the apex predators of Silurian seas and freshwaters, some reaching 2.5 meters. This specimen, approximately 430 million years old, preserves th…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Eurypterids — 'sea scorpions' — were the apex predators of Silurian seas and freshwaters, some reaching 2.5 meters. This specimen, approximately 430 million years old, preserves the articulated appendages and paddle-like swimming legs of an arthropod lineage that ruled for 200 million years before dying in the Permian extinction. Their nearest living relatives are horseshoe crabs.

short_description: Walliserops trifurcatus is palaeontology's most improbable arthropod: a trilobite with a long, ornate trident growing from its head. The function of this structure remains debated …
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Walliserops trifurcatus is palaeontology's most improbable arthropod: a trilobite with a long, ornate trident growing from its head. The function of this structure remains debated — competition display? Prey manipulation? — but its geometric elaboration suggests evolution's willingness to experiment. Only a few hundred specimens are known. Each is a unanswered question in stone.

name: Trilobite — Enrolled Phacops — Field-Collected
short_description: Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual c…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual calcite lenses each oriented to minimize spherical aberration, represent an optical system that evolution independently re-discovered in modern compound eyes. The Moroccan Sahara was once a shallow tropical sea.

name: Coprolite — Predator Specimen
short_description: Coprolites are fossils of feces, and they are paleontology's most underappreciated resource. Analysis of what was consumed (bone fragments, scales, feathers, plant material) tells …
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Coprolites are fossils of feces, and they are paleontology's most underappreciated resource. Analysis of what was consumed (bone fragments, scales, feathers, plant material) tells us more about ancient diets than bones alone. Montana's Judith River Formation (Cretaceous) yields coprolites from large theropods, identifiable by crushed bone content, size, and context.

This specimen originates from Judith River Formation, Montana, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian vertebra is diagnostic: the distinctive hourglass shape, with a central channel for the notochord, identifies an animal whose lineage includes the long-necked forms that inspired the Loch Ness myth.

name: Fish Fossil — Knightia eocaena (Twin Plate) — Field-Collected
short_description: Knightia is the Wyoming state fossil and the most commonly collected fossil vertebrate in the world — but a twin plate, two fish preserved together, suggests a specific depositiona…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Knightia is the Wyoming state fossil and the most commonly collected fossil vertebrate in the world — but a twin plate, two fish preserved together, suggests a specific depositional event: a temperature fluctuation or algal bloom that killed multiple fish simultaneously. Their exact positions at death were preserved by the rapid burial in carbonate sediment.

This specimen originates from Green River Formation, Wyoming, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Fish Fossil — Green River Formation
short_description: The Green River Formation — Eocene lake beds in Wyoming, Colorado, and Utah — is one of Earth's great fossil deposits. Fish are preserved in such fine-grained varve (annual) layers…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. The Green River Formation — Eocene lake beds in Wyoming, Colorado, and Utah — is one of Earth's great fossil deposits. Fish are preserved in such fine-grained varve (annual) layers that individual scales are visible under a loupe, and the stomach contents of some specimens can be identified. These Eocene fish died approximately 50 million years ago, on a calm day in a subtropical lake.

name: Dinosaur Tooth — Carcharodontosaurus — Museum Replica
short_description: Carcharodontosaurus — 'shark-toothed lizard' — was one of the largest theropod dinosaurs, rivaling T. rex in body size. Its blade-like serrated teeth, up to 8 centimeters long, cou…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Carcharodontosaurus — 'shark-toothed lizard' — was one of the largest theropod dinosaurs, rivaling T. rex in body size. Its blade-like serrated teeth, up to 8 centimeters long, could slice through flesh with a lateral shaking motion. This specimen from Morocco's Late Cretaceous Kem Kem Formation is 95 million years old.

name: Trilobite — Enrolled Phacops — Display Grade
short_description: Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual c…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual calcite lenses each oriented to minimize spherical aberration, represent an optical system that evolution independently re-discovered in modern compound eyes. The Moroccan Sahara was once a shallow tropical sea.

name: Spider in Amber — Burma
short_description: Burmese amber's 99-million-year age places its inclusions squarely in the Cretaceous — contemporary with apex predator dinosaurs. Spiders from this period belong to lineages that l…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Burmese amber's 99-million-year age places its inclusions squarely in the Cretaceous — contemporary with apex predator dinosaurs. Spiders from this period belong to lineages that look remarkably similar to modern relatives, a testament to the effectiveness of the spider body plan. Some Burmese amber spiders have been found in silk-wrapped prey — hunting behavior frozen at the moment of success.

This specimen originates from Hukawng Valley, Myanmar, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Shark Teeth — Odontaspis Cluster — Display Grade
locality: Lee Creek Mine, North Carolina, USA
short_description: Lee Creek's Miocene (5–20 million years ago) phosphate deposits are among North America's richest fossil marine vertebrate sites. The sand tiger shark Odontaspis produced multi-roo…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Lee Creek's Miocene (5–20 million years ago) phosphate deposits are among North America's richest fossil marine vertebrate sites. The sand tiger shark Odontaspis produced multi-rooted, needle-like teeth that formed in conveyor-belt succession — sharks shed and replace thousands of teeth in a lifetime. A cluster plate shows this biological abundance fossilized.

This specimen originates from Lee Creek Mine, North Carolina, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Sea Urchin — Micraster — Prepared Specimen
short_description: The white chalk of southern England — the White Cliffs of Dover — was deposited between 70–100 million years ago as a thick ooze of coccolithophore shells (single-celled algae) on …
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. The white chalk of southern England — the White Cliffs of Dover — was deposited between 70–100 million years ago as a thick ooze of coccolithophore shells (single-celled algae) on a warm, shallow sea floor. Within it, heart urchins like Micraster were buried and preserved in three dimensions, their test intact. British farmers once called them 'fairy loaves' and placed them on hearths for luck.

name: Crinoid Stem Section — Polished — Field-Collected
short_description: Crinoids — 'sea lilies' — are filter-feeding echinoderms that have existed for 480 million years. Their multi-jointed stems, when polished in cross-section, reveal a perfectly five…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Crinoids — 'sea lilies' — are filter-feeding echinoderms that have existed for 480 million years. Their multi-jointed stems, when polished in cross-section, reveal a perfectly five-fold symmetric pattern that looks like a star or flower. Entire Mississippian limestone beds are composed of little else: a living carpet of these animals covered Paleozoic sea floors to horizon.

This specimen originates from Mississippian Formation, Indiana, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Mammoth Molar — Display Grade
short_description: The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Plei…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Pleistocene megafauna remains up to 1.8 million years old. A mammoth molar's ridged enamel plates, evolved for grinding tough grasses on frigid steppes, is still recognizable: evolution's answer to permafrost diets.

This specimen originates from North Sea Beds, Netherlands, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. short_description: Spinosaurus aegyptiacus — 95–100 million years old — may have been the largest predatory dinosaur that ever lived, longer than T. rex and adapted for catching fish with conical, un…

name: Coprolite — Predator Specimen — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: Coprolites are fossils of feces, and they are paleontology's most underappreciated resource. Analysis of what was consumed (bone fragments, scales, feathers, plant material) tells …
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Coprolites are fossils of feces, and they are paleontology's most underappreciated resource. Analysis of what was consumed (bone fragments, scales, feathers, plant material) tells us more about ancient diets than bones alone. Montana's Judith River Formation (Cretaceous) yields coprolites from large theropods, identifiable by crushed bone content, size, and context.

This specimen originates from Judith River Formation, Montana, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. short_description: Social insects — ants, bees, termites — evolved their complex societies in the Eocene or earlier, and Baltic amber preserves them in behavioral poses: workers, queens, males, and s…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Social insects — ants, bees, termites — evolved their complex societies in the Eocene or earlier, and Baltic amber preserves them in behavioral poses: workers, queens, males, and sometimes entire colonies trapped together. An ant preserved in amber carries all the social information of its species frozen 44 million years ago. Evolution runs on deep time.

This specimen originates from Baltic Region, Poland/Russia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Megalodon Tooth Replica — Detailed — Small Specimen
short_description: Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking in ocean trenches, as popularized fiction suggests). These precise casts of actual 7-centimeter teeth from South Carolina show the serrations clearly. The original sharks are gone; the geometry of their teeth endures.

This specimen originates from South Carolina, USA (cast from original), one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Megalodon Tooth Replica — Detailed — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking in ocean trenches, as popularized fiction suggests). These precise casts of actual 7-centimeter teeth from South Carolina show the serrations clearly. The original sharks are gone; the geometry of their teeth endures.

name: Petrified Wood — Araucarioxylon — Small Specimen
short_description: 228-million-year-old trees from the Triassic floodplains of what would become Arizona were buried in volcanic ash, silicified over millions of years, then exposed by erosion. The o…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. 228-million-year-old trees from the Triassic floodplains of what would become Arizona were buried in volcanic ash, silicified over millions of years, then exposed by erosion. The original wood cells are replaced by quartz or chalcedony in precise detail — annual rings, cellular structure, and even fungal damage preserved in stone. These trees predated both mammals and birds.

name: Crinoid Stem Section — Polished — Display Grade
short_description: Crinoids — 'sea lilies' — are filter-feeding echinoderms that have existed for 480 million years. Their multi-jointed stems, when polished in cross-section, reveal a perfectly five…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Crinoids — 'sea lilies' — are filter-feeding echinoderms that have existed for 480 million years. Their multi-jointed stems, when polished in cross-section, reveal a perfectly five-fold symmetric pattern that looks like a star or flower. Entire Mississippian limestone beds are composed of little else: a living carpet of these animals covered Paleozoic sea floors to horizon.

name: Glyptodon Osteoderms — Display Grade
short_description: Glyptodon — an armadillo the size of a Volkswagen Beetle — roamed South America until approximately 10,000 years ago. Its rigid shell was composed of thousands of interlocking oste…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Glyptodon — an armadillo the size of a Volkswagen Beetle — roamed South America until approximately 10,000 years ago. Its rigid shell was composed of thousands of interlocking osteoderms (bone plates) hexagonal in pattern, like the shell of a turtle but evolved independently in armadillo relatives. Early humans likely hunted glyptodons; some shells show evidence of having been used as shelters.

name: Trilobite — Walliserops trifurcatus (Trident) — Field-Collected
short_description: Walliserops trifurcatus is palaeontology's most improbable arthropod: a trilobite with a long, ornate trident growing from its head. The function of this structure remains debated …
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Walliserops trifurcatus is palaeontology's most improbable arthropod: a trilobite with a long, ornate trident growing from its head. The function of this structure remains debated — competition display? Prey manipulation? — but its geometric elaboration suggests evolution's willingness to experiment. Only a few hundred specimens are known. Each is a unanswered question in stone.

name: Fish Fossil — Knightia eocaena (Twin Plate) — Display Grade
short_description: Knightia is the Wyoming state fossil and the most commonly collected fossil vertebrate in the world — but a twin plate, two fish preserved together, suggests a specific depositiona…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Knightia is the Wyoming state fossil and the most commonly collected fossil vertebrate in the world — but a twin plate, two fish preserved together, suggests a specific depositional event: a temperature fluctuation or algal bloom that killed multiple fish simultaneously. Their exact positions at death were preserved by the rapid burial in carbonate sediment.

name: Nautiloid — Cyrtoceras Orthocerid — Small Specimen
short_description: Straight-shelled nautiloids — orthocerids — were among the apex predators of Ordovician and Silurian seas, reaching several meters in length. These Moroccan specimens, 380–420 mill…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Straight-shelled nautiloids — orthocerids — were among the apex predators of Ordovician and Silurian seas, reaching several meters in length. These Moroccan specimens, 380–420 million years old, are polished to reveal the internal septa (dividing walls) that the living animal used for buoyancy control, flooding chambers with gas or water as needed. Living nautiluses still use this mechanism.

name: Brachiopod Cluster — Spirifer — Prepared Specimen
short_description: Brachiopods look superficially like clams but are unrelated — a classic example of convergent evolution. They dominated seafloors for 300 million years. Devonian Spirifer brachiopo…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Brachiopods look superficially like clams but are unrelated — a classic example of convergent evolution. They dominated seafloors for 300 million years. Devonian Spirifer brachiopods from New York state show the distinctive 'wings' and delicate ribbing of a filter-feeding animal that lived in dense communities on warm, shallow seafloors. The Devonian mass extinction largely ended their dominance.

name: Mosasaur Tooth on Matrix — Prepared Specimen
short_description: Mosasaurs ruled Cretaceous seas for 23 million years — massive predatory lizards (not dinosaurs) up to 17 meters long, with double-hinged jaws that could swallow large prey whole. …
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Mosasaurs ruled Cretaceous seas for 23 million years — massive predatory lizards (not dinosaurs) up to 17 meters long, with double-hinged jaws that could swallow large prey whole. This tooth, still embedded in jaw matrix, is from the latest Cretaceous (~70 million years ago). These animals and the non-avian dinosaurs went extinct simultaneously in the end-Cretaceous mass extinction.

name: Horseshoe Crab — Limulus — Prepared Specimen
short_description: The horseshoe crab lineage has changed so little in 450 million years that Limulus today is nearly identical to Silurian relatives. This Eocene specimen from the Green River Format…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The horseshoe crab lineage has changed so little in 450 million years that Limulus today is nearly identical to Silurian relatives. This Eocene specimen from the Green River Formation (~50 million years old) is a near-perfect preservation of a 'living fossil' mid-career — the animal looked essentially like this long before humans existed, and still does. Time is a patient sculptor.

name: Plesiosaurian Vertebra — Field-Collected
short_description: The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. The Jurassic coast of Dorset — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — erodes continuously, releasing marine reptile remains from Kimmeridgian clays (155 million years old). A plesiosaurian vertebra is diagnostic: the distinctive hourglass shape, with a central channel for the notochord, identifies an animal whose lineage includes the long-necked forms that inspired the Loch Ness myth.

name: Ammonite — Polished Section — Museum Replica
short_description: Cut and polished, an ammonite's internal chambers reveal the logarithmic spiral that mathematicians called the 'golden ratio' centuries before they understood its name. The iridesc…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Cut and polished, an ammonite's internal chambers reveal the logarithmic spiral that mathematicians called the 'golden ratio' centuries before they understood its name. The iridescent nacreous layer — preserved aragonite from the original shell — creates colors that shift with viewing angle. These Cretaceous cephalopods died 66 million years ago; their shells are more beautiful than they could have imagined.

name: Ammonite — Opalized — Prepared Specimen
locality: Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia
short_description: When an ammonite shell dissolves and is replaced atom by atom by silica spheres of uniform size, the result is an opalized fossil — a creature's geometry wearing a gem's colors. Li…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. When an ammonite shell dissolves and is replaced atom by atom by silica spheres of uniform size, the result is an opalized fossil — a creature's geometry wearing a gem's colors. Lightning Ridge ammonites are among the most arresting objects in palaeontology: the form of something 100 million years old, in the colors of a fire.

short_description: Eurypterids — 'sea scorpions' — were the apex predators of Silurian seas and freshwaters, some reaching 2.5 meters. This specimen, approximately 430 million years old, preserves th…

Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Eurypterids — 'sea scorpions' — were the apex predators of Silurian seas and freshwaters, some reaching 2.5 meters. This specimen, approximately 430 million years old, preserves the articulated appendages and paddle-like swimming legs of an arthropod lineage that ruled for 200 million years before dying in the Permian extinction. Their nearest living relatives are horseshoe crabs.

name: Mammoth Molar — Museum Replica
short_description: The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Plei…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Pleistocene megafauna remains up to 1.8 million years old. A mammoth molar's ridged enamel plates, evolved for grinding tough grasses on frigid steppes, is still recognizable: evolution's answer to permafrost diets.

name: Insect in Amber — Ant Cluster — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: Social insects — ants, bees, termites — evolved their complex societies in the Eocene or earlier, and Baltic amber preserves them in behavioral poses: workers, queens, males, and s…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Social insects — ants, bees, termites — evolved their complex societies in the Eocene or earlier, and Baltic amber preserves them in behavioral poses: workers, queens, males, and sometimes entire colonies trapped together. An ant preserved in amber carries all the social information of its species frozen 44 million years ago. Evolution runs on deep time.

name: Trilobite — Walliserops trifurcatus (Trident) — Museum Replica
short_description: Walliserops trifurcatus is palaeontology's most improbable arthropod: a trilobite with a long, ornate trident growing from its head. The function of this structure remains debated …
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Walliserops trifurcatus is palaeontology's most improbable arthropod: a trilobite with a long, ornate trident growing from its head. The function of this structure remains debated — competition display? Prey manipulation? — but its geometric elaboration suggests evolution's willingness to experiment. Only a few hundred specimens are known. Each is a unanswered question in stone.

name: Insect in Amber — Ant Cluster — Field-Collected
short_description: Social insects — ants, bees, termites — evolved their complex societies in the Eocene or earlier, and Baltic amber preserves them in behavioral poses: workers, queens, males, and s…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Social insects — ants, bees, termites — evolved their complex societies in the Eocene or earlier, and Baltic amber preserves them in behavioral poses: workers, queens, males, and sometimes entire colonies trapped together. An ant preserved in amber carries all the social information of its species frozen 44 million years ago. Evolution runs on deep time.

name: Fern Fossil — Pecopteris — Prepared Specimen
short_description: Mazon Creek concretions — iron-carbonate nodules from Carboniferous coal measures — have yielded some of the most diverse fossil flora on Earth. Crack one open and find a perfect f…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Mazon Creek concretions — iron-carbonate nodules from Carboniferous coal measures — have yielded some of the most diverse fossil flora on Earth. Crack one open and find a perfect fern frond, preserved in three dimensions, from a coal swamp 309 million years ago. This specimen predates the dinosaurs by 75 million years. Carboniferous forests built the coal we burned for the industrial revolution.

name: Trace Fossil — Dinosaur Footprint Cast
short_description: The Connecticut Valley contains one of North America's richest trace fossil records — Triassic dinosaurs walking across mudflats that dried and lithified. Trace fossils record beha…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. The Connecticut Valley contains one of North America's richest trace fossil records — Triassic dinosaurs walking across mudflats that dried and lithified. Trace fossils record behavior, not bodies: the weight distribution of an animal in mid-stride, its pace length, whether it was running. This three-toed track was made 200 million years ago by an early theropod whose skeleton has never been found.

name: Megalodon Tooth Replica — Detailed — Prepared Specimen
short_description: Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Carcharocles megalodon — possibly the largest predatory shark to have ever lived — reached perhaps 18 meters and went extinct approximately 3.6 million years ago (not still lurking in ocean trenches, as popularized fiction suggests). These precise casts of actual 7-centimeter teeth from South Carolina show the serrations clearly. The original sharks are gone; the geometry of their teeth endures.

This specimen originates from South Carolina, USA (cast from original), one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Ammonite — Hildoceras bifrons — Field-Collected
short_description: Whitby's Jurassic shales have yielded ammonites since the Anglo-Saxon era — local legend turned them into petrified snakes (they lacked the heads, so locals carved them on). The py…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Whitby's Jurassic shales have yielded ammonites since the Anglo-Saxon era — local legend turned them into petrified snakes (they lacked the heads, so locals carved them on). The pyritized Hildoceras bifrons specimens, 180 million years old, show the ribbed sutures that distinguish each ammonite genus. Yorkshire's coast is actively eroding, releasing new specimens each winter storm.

name: Fish Fossil — Green River Formation — Prepared Specimen
short_description: The Green River Formation — Eocene lake beds in Wyoming, Colorado, and Utah — is one of Earth's great fossil deposits. Fish are preserved in such fine-grained varve (annual) layers…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The Green River Formation — Eocene lake beds in Wyoming, Colorado, and Utah — is one of Earth's great fossil deposits. Fish are preserved in such fine-grained varve (annual) layers that individual scales are visible under a loupe, and the stomach contents of some specimens can be identified. These Eocene fish died approximately 50 million years ago, on a calm day in a subtropical lake.

name: Petrified Wood — Araucarioxylon
short_description: 228-million-year-old trees from the Triassic floodplains of what would become Arizona were buried in volcanic ash, silicified over millions of years, then exposed by erosion. The o…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. 228-million-year-old trees from the Triassic floodplains of what would become Arizona were buried in volcanic ash, silicified over millions of years, then exposed by erosion. The original wood cells are replaced by quartz or chalcedony in precise detail — annual rings, cellular structure, and even fungal damage preserved in stone. These trees predated both mammals and birds.

This specimen originates from Petrified Forest, Arizona, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Brachiopod Cluster — Spirifer — Museum Replica
short_description: Brachiopods look superficially like clams but are unrelated — a classic example of convergent evolution. They dominated seafloors for 300 million years. Devonian Spirifer brachiopo…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Brachiopods look superficially like clams but are unrelated — a classic example of convergent evolution. They dominated seafloors for 300 million years. Devonian Spirifer brachiopods from New York state show the distinctive 'wings' and delicate ribbing of a filter-feeding animal that lived in dense communities on warm, shallow seafloors. The Devonian mass extinction largely ended their dominance.

This specimen originates from Devonian limestone, New York, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Mammoth Molar — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Plei…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The North Sea was dry land during the last ice age — mammoths, woolly rhinos, and early humans walked where fishing boats now drag. Sand and gravel dredging regularly recovers Pleistocene megafauna remains up to 1.8 million years old. A mammoth molar's ridged enamel plates, evolved for grinding tough grasses on frigid steppes, is still recognizable: evolution's answer to permafrost diets.

name: Plant Fossil — Sigillaria
short_description: Sigillaria was a coal-swamp tree reaching 30 meters, with a bark pattern of hexagonal leaf scars that looks like a reptile's scales. The Carboniferous forests of 310 million years …
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Sigillaria was a coal-swamp tree reaching 30 meters, with a bark pattern of hexagonal leaf scars that looks like a reptile's scales. The Carboniferous forests of 310 million years ago were composed of such trees alongside giant horsetails and ferns — all now extinct genera. Their compressed remains are literally the coal seams that powered the industrial revolution.

This specimen originates from Carboniferous, Saarland, Germany, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Ammonite — Opalized — Exceptional Preservation
locality: Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia
short_description: When an ammonite shell dissolves and is replaced atom by atom by silica spheres of uniform size, the result is an opalized fossil — a creature's geometry wearing a gem's colors. Li…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. When an ammonite shell dissolves and is replaced atom by atom by silica spheres of uniform size, the result is an opalized fossil — a creature's geometry wearing a gem's colors. Lightning Ridge ammonites are among the most arresting objects in palaeontology: the form of something 100 million years old, in the colors of a fire.

name: Trilobite — Enrolled Phacops — Small Specimen
short_description: Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual c…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Phacops enrolled into a defensive ball the moment danger threatened — 380 million years ago in a Devonian sea. This one never unrolled. The calcified eyes, composed of individual calcite lenses each oriented to minimize spherical aberration, represent an optical system that evolution independently re-discovered in modern compound eyes. The Moroccan Sahara was once a shallow tropical sea.

This specimen originates from Hamar Laghdad, Erfoud, Morocco, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Shark Teeth — Odontaspis Cluster — Museum Replica
locality: Lee Creek Mine, North Carolina, USA
short_description: Lee Creek's Miocene (5–20 million years ago) phosphate deposits are among North America's richest fossil marine vertebrate sites. The sand tiger shark Odontaspis produced multi-roo…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Lee Creek's Miocene (5–20 million years ago) phosphate deposits are among North America's richest fossil marine vertebrate sites. The sand tiger shark Odontaspis produced multi-rooted, needle-like teeth that formed in conveyor-belt succession — sharks shed and replace thousands of teeth in a lifetime. A cluster plate shows this biological abundance fossilized.

This specimen originates from Lee Creek Mine, North Carolina, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Dinosaur Tooth — Carcharodontosaurus — Display Grade
short_description: Carcharodontosaurus — 'shark-toothed lizard' — was one of the largest theropod dinosaurs, rivaling T. rex in body size. Its blade-like serrated teeth, up to 8 centimeters long, cou…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Carcharodontosaurus — 'shark-toothed lizard' — was one of the largest theropod dinosaurs, rivaling T. rex in body size. Its blade-like serrated teeth, up to 8 centimeters long, could slice through flesh with a lateral shaking motion. This specimen from Morocco's Late Cretaceous Kem Kem Formation is 95 million years old.

This specimen originates from Kem Kem Formation, Morocco, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Mosasaurs ruled Cretaceous seas for 23 million years — massive predatory lizards (not dinosaurs) up to 17 meters long, with double-hinged jaws that could swallow large prey whole. name: Sea Urchin — Micraster — Small Specimen
short_description: The white chalk of southern England — the White Cliffs of Dover — was deposited between 70–100 million years ago as a thick ooze of coccolithophore shells (single-celled algae) on …
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. The white chalk of southern England — the White Cliffs of Dover — was deposited between 70–100 million years ago as a thick ooze of coccolithophore shells (single-celled algae) on a warm, shallow sea floor. Within it, heart urchins like Micraster were buried and preserved in three dimensions, their test intact. British farmers once called them 'fairy loaves' and placed them on hearths for luck.

name: Plant Fossil — Sigillaria — Exceptional Preservation
short_description: Sigillaria was a coal-swamp tree reaching 30 meters, with a bark pattern of hexagonal leaf scars that looks like a reptile's scales. The Carboniferous forests of 310 million years …
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Sigillaria was a coal-swamp tree reaching 30 meters, with a bark pattern of hexagonal leaf scars that looks like a reptile's scales. The Carboniferous forests of 310 million years ago were composed of such trees alongside giant horsetails and ferns — all now extinct genera. Their compressed remains are literally the coal seams that powered the industrial revolution.

This specimen originates from Carboniferous, Saarland, Germany, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Ammonite — Polished Section — Field-Collected
short_description: Cut and polished, an ammonite's internal chambers reveal the logarithmic spiral that mathematicians called the 'golden ratio' centuries before they understood its name. The iridesc…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Cut and polished, an ammonite's internal chambers reveal the logarithmic spiral that mathematicians called the 'golden ratio' centuries before they understood its name. The iridescent nacreous layer — preserved aragonite from the original shell — creates colors that shift with viewing angle. These Cretaceous cephalopods died 66 million years ago; their shells are more beautiful than they could have imagined.

name: Horseshoe Crab — Limulus — Small Specimen
short_description: The horseshoe crab lineage has changed so little in 450 million years that Limulus today is nearly identical to Silurian relatives. This Eocene specimen from the Green River Format…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The horseshoe crab lineage has changed so little in 450 million years that Limulus today is nearly identical to Silurian relatives. This Eocene specimen from the Green River Formation (~50 million years old) is a near-perfect preservation of a 'living fossil' mid-career — the animal looked essentially like this long before humans existed, and still does. Time is a patient sculptor.

name: Eurypterid — Sea Scorpion — Small Specimen
short_description: Eurypterids — 'sea scorpions' — were the apex predators of Silurian seas and freshwaters, some reaching 2.5 meters. This specimen, approximately 430 million years old, preserves th…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Eurypterids — 'sea scorpions' — were the apex predators of Silurian seas and freshwaters, some reaching 2.5 meters. This specimen, approximately 430 million years old, preserves the articulated appendages and paddle-like swimming legs of an arthropod lineage that ruled for 200 million years before dying in the Permian extinction. Their nearest living relatives are horseshoe crabs.

name: Ammonite — Opalized — Field-Collected
locality: Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia
short_description: When an ammonite shell dissolves and is replaced atom by atom by silica spheres of uniform size, the result is an opalized fossil — a creature's geometry wearing a gem's colors. Li…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. When an ammonite shell dissolves and is replaced atom by atom by silica spheres of uniform size, the result is an opalized fossil — a creature's geometry wearing a gem's colors. Lightning Ridge ammonites are among the most arresting objects in palaeontology: the form of something 100 million years old, in the colors of a fire.

name: Ammonite — Hildoceras bifrons
short_description: Whitby's Jurassic shales have yielded ammonites since the Anglo-Saxon era — local legend turned them into petrified snakes (they lacked the heads, so locals carved them on). The py…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Whitby's Jurassic shales have yielded ammonites since the Anglo-Saxon era — local legend turned them into petrified snakes (they lacked the heads, so locals carved them on). The pyritized Hildoceras bifrons specimens, 180 million years old, show the ribbed sutures that distinguish each ammonite genus. Yorkshire's coast is actively eroding, releasing new specimens each winter storm.

This specimen originates from Whitby, North Yorkshire, England, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Lunar Meteorite — Mare Basalt — Small Individual
short_description: These were once rocks on the lunar maria — the dark volcanic plains visible with the naked eye from Earth. An asteroid impact on the Moon ejected this material with enough velocity…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. These were once rocks on the lunar maria — the dark volcanic plains visible with the naked eye from Earth. An asteroid impact on the Moon ejected this material with enough velocity to escape lunar gravity. It traveled through space for millions of years before entering Earth's atmosphere. You can hold the Moon.

name: Iron Meteorite — Toluca — Polished Face
short_description: The Toluca iron was first reported in 1776 and became one of the most-distributed meteorites in museums worldwide. Its Widmanstätten pattern is particularly coarse — wide bands ind…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The Toluca iron was first reported in 1776 and became one of the most-distributed meteorites in museums worldwide. Its Widmanstätten pattern is particularly coarse — wide bands indicating slow cooling from a large parent body. The fall likely occurred before recorded history; Aztec artifacts of meteoric iron have been found in the region.

name: Pallasite Meteorite — Olivine in Metal
short_description: Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic collision. The result — golden olivine crystals floating in a metallic matrix, backlit like stained glass — is one of the most beautiful materials in the solar system. Brahin pallasites show exceptional olivine clarity.

name: Gibeon Iron Meteorite — Etched Slice — Fragment
short_description: The Widmanstätten pattern — those angular geometric bands revealed by acid etching — took one million years per millimeter to form as the Gibeon parent body cooled in the vacuum of…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The Widmanstätten pattern — those angular geometric bands revealed by acid etching — took one million years per millimeter to form as the Gibeon parent body cooled in the vacuum of space at approximately one degree per million years. You cannot fake this. It is the slowest signature in the universe. These irons fell across 275×100 km of Namibia, an ancient strewn field from a collision long before Earth had life.

This specimen originates from Gibeon, Hardap Region, Namibia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Imilac Pallasite
short_description: Imilac pallasites from Chile's Atacama Desert show extraordinary olivine crystal size and clarity. The dry desert preserved the metal matrix against weathering while the olivine cr…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Imilac pallasites from Chile's Atacama Desert show extraordinary olivine crystal size and clarity. The dry desert preserved the metal matrix against weathering while the olivine crystals — gem-quality peridot in composition — remained intact. Cut and polished thin sections transmit light through the olivine in warm yellow-green, while the etched metal shows Widmanstätten pattern. It is both geology and jewelry.

This specimen originates from Atacama Desert, Chile, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Moldavite — Czech Tektite
short_description: 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravi…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravia. Moldavite is that impact glass — each piece formed in less than a second from vaporized European crust, then shaped by supersonic flight before cooling in free fall. The green color comes from iron.

This specimen originates from Bohemia and Moravia, Czech Republic, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Seymchan Pallasite — Fragment
short_description: Seymchan was originally classified as an iron meteorite when first found in 1967; only later were olivine-bearing sections discovered. The Seymchan parent body was therefore caught…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Seymchan was originally classified as an iron meteorite when first found in 1967; only later were olivine-bearing sections discovered. The Seymchan parent body was therefore caught in transition from iron meteorite to pallasite — a snapshot of asteroid differentiation in progress. It is one of meteorite science's most interesting classification ambiguities.
