name: Campo del Cielo Iron Meteorite — Small Individual
short_description: Campo del Cielo — 'Field of the Sky' — fell approximately 4,500 years ago across the Gran Chaco. Indigenous peoples knew the crater field; the first European report came in 1576 fr…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Campo del Cielo — 'Field of the Sky' — fell approximately 4,500 years ago across the Gran Chaco. Indigenous peoples knew the crater field; the first European report came in 1576 from a Spanish governor who noted 'a mass of iron.' The fall produced multiple craters; the combined mass recovered exceeds 100 tonnes, making it one of the world's largest recorded meteorite fields.

name: Pallasite Meteorite — Olivine in Metal — Polished Face
short_description: Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic collision. The result — golden olivine crystals floating in a metallic matrix, backlit like stained glass — is one of the most beautiful materials in the solar system. Brahin pallasites show exceptional olivine clarity.

This specimen originates from Brahin, Gomel Region, Belarus, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.

name: Iron Meteorite — Toluca
short_description: The Toluca iron was first reported in 1776 and became one of the most-distributed meteorites in museums worldwide. Its Widmanstätten pattern is particularly coarse — wide bands ind…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. The Toluca iron was first reported in 1776 and became one of the most-distributed meteorites in museums worldwide. Its Widmanstätten pattern is particularly coarse — wide bands indicating slow cooling from a large parent body. The fall likely occurred before recorded history; Aztec artifacts of meteoric iron have been found in the region.

This specimen originates from Toluca Valley, Mexico, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Lunar Meteorite — Mare Basalt — Etched Section
short_description: These were once rocks on the lunar maria — the dark volcanic plains visible with the naked eye from Earth. An asteroid impact on the Moon ejected this material with enough velocity…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. These were once rocks on the lunar maria — the dark volcanic plains visible with the naked eye from Earth. An asteroid impact on the Moon ejected this material with enough velocity to escape lunar gravity. It traveled through space for millions of years before entering Earth's atmosphere. You can hold the Moon.

name: Carbonaceous Chondrite — CM2 — Oriented Individual
short_description: Carbonaceous chondrites are the most primitive meteorites — some contain presolar grains older than the solar system itself, including nanodiamonds and silicon carbide formed in ot…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Carbonaceous chondrites are the most primitive meteorites — some contain presolar grains older than the solar system itself, including nanodiamonds and silicon carbide formed in other star systems before our Sun ignited. CM2 chondrites also contain amino acids, complex organics, and water-bearing minerals. The building blocks of life arrived by meteorite: this is no longer a metaphor.

This specimen originates from Northwest Africa, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Sikhote-Alin Individual — Shrapnel — Etched Section
short_description: On February 12, 1947, witnesses in the Sikhote-Alin mountains saw a fireball brighter than the sun at 10:38 AM. The iron meteorite fragmented as it decelerated, producing a shower …
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. On February 12, 1947, witnesses in the Sikhote-Alin mountains saw a fireball brighter than the sun at 10:38 AM. The iron meteorite fragmented as it decelerated, producing a shower of sharp, angular shrapnel individuals in 23 craters across 2 km². These regmaglypts — thumb-print-like depressions from atmospheric ablation — were literally sculpted by air molecules at 15 km/s.

name: Moldavite — Czech Tektite — Polished Face
short_description: 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravi…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravia. Moldavite is that impact glass — each piece formed in less than a second from vaporized European crust, then shaped by supersonic flight before cooling in free fall. The green color comes from iron.

This specimen originates from Bohemia and Moravia, Czech Republic, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Libyan Desert Glass
short_description: Libyan desert glass — found in a 6,500 km² strewn field — is the purest natural silica glass on Earth. Its formation 29 million years ago involved temperatures exceeding 1600°C, su…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Libyan desert glass — found in a 6,500 km² strewn field — is the purest natural silica glass on Earth. Its formation 29 million years ago involved temperatures exceeding 1600°C, suggesting an airburst or impact-generated thermal event so intense it flash-melted the Saharan sand. A carved Libyan desert glass scarab was found in Tutankhamun's pectoral — ancient Egyptians recognized it as extraordinary.

This specimen originates from Great Sand Sea, Libya/Egypt, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

collector_notes: Handle with care; crystal terminations are irreplaceable once damaged.

name: Darwin Glass — Tasmania — Large Slice
short_description: Darwin glass formed 816,000 years ago when a meteorite struck western Tasmania, creating a 1.2-kilometer crater. The glass — pale green, pale grey, white, and bubbly — was melted f…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Darwin glass formed 816,000 years ago when a meteorite struck western Tasmania, creating a 1.2-kilometer crater. The glass — pale green, pale grey, white, and bubbly — was melted from local quartzite and dolerite. Unlike moldavite's aerodynamic shaping, Darwin glass shows the chaos of a close-in explosion: twisted, vesicular, sometimes bubbly with trapped gas.

This specimen originates from Darwin Crater, Tasmania, Australia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Imilac Pallasite — Fragment
short_description: Imilac pallasites from Chile's Atacama Desert show extraordinary olivine crystal size and clarity. The dry desert preserved the metal matrix against weathering while the olivine cr…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Imilac pallasites from Chile's Atacama Desert show extraordinary olivine crystal size and clarity. The dry desert preserved the metal matrix against weathering while the olivine crystals — gem-quality peridot in composition — remained intact. Cut and polished thin sections transmit light through the olivine in warm yellow-green, while the etched metal shows Widmanstätten pattern. It is both geology and jewelry.

This specimen originates from Atacama Desert, Chile, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Martian Meteorite — Shergottite — Oriented Individual
short_description: The ultimate provenance story: this rock formed on Mars in a volcanic eruption approximately 180 million years ago. A massive impact blasted it from the Martian surface with enough…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. The ultimate provenance story: this rock formed on Mars in a volcanic eruption approximately 180 million years ago. A massive impact blasted it from the Martian surface with enough force to achieve escape velocity. It traveled through interplanetary space before landing on Earth. Martian origin is verified by trapped gas matching Viking lander measurements of the Martian atmosphere.

This specimen originates from Northwest Africa (NWA 2975 type), one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Gibeon Iron Meteorite — Etched Slice
short_description: The Widmanstätten pattern — those angular geometric bands revealed by acid etching — took one million years per millimeter to form as the Gibeon parent body cooled in the vacuum of…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. The Widmanstätten pattern — those angular geometric bands revealed by acid etching — took one million years per millimeter to form as the Gibeon parent body cooled in the vacuum of space at approximately one degree per million years. You cannot fake this. It is the slowest signature in the universe. These irons fell across 275×100 km of Namibia, an ancient strewn field from a collision long before Earth had life.

This specimen originates from Gibeon, Hardap Region, Namibia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Seymchan Pallasite — Small Individual
short_description: Seymchan was originally classified as an iron meteorite when first found in 1967; only later were olivine-bearing sections discovered. The Seymchan parent body was therefore caught…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Seymchan was originally classified as an iron meteorite when first found in 1967; only later were olivine-bearing sections discovered. The Seymchan parent body was therefore caught in transition from iron meteorite to pallasite — a snapshot of asteroid differentiation in progress. It is one of meteorite science's most interesting classification ambiguities.

name: Campo del Cielo Iron Meteorite — Etched Section
short_description: Campo del Cielo — 'Field of the Sky' — fell approximately 4,500 years ago across the Gran Chaco. Indigenous peoples knew the crater field; the first European report came in 1576 fr…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Campo del Cielo — 'Field of the Sky' — fell approximately 4,500 years ago across the Gran Chaco. Indigenous peoples knew the crater field; the first European report came in 1576 from a Spanish governor who noted 'a mass of iron.' The fall produced multiple craters; the combined mass recovered exceeds 100 tonnes, making it one of the world's largest recorded meteorite fields.

This specimen originates from Chaco Province, Argentina, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Chondrite Meteorite — L4 Type — Oriented Individual
short_description: Chondrites are the oldest solid matter in the solar system — 4.56 billion years old, predating Earth by 30–40 million years. Chondrules — spherical beads visible in a fresh cut — w…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Chondrites are the oldest solid matter in the solar system — 4.56 billion years old, predating Earth by 30–40 million years. Chondrules — spherical beads visible in a fresh cut — were flash-melted from nebular dust by poorly understood events in the solar nebula. The 'L' designation means low iron; the '4' means moderate metamorphism. This is literal building material from the protoplanetary disk.

name: Sikhote-Alin Individual — Shrapnel — Fragment
short_description: On February 12, 1947, witnesses in the Sikhote-Alin mountains saw a fireball brighter than the sun at 10:38 AM. The iron meteorite fragmented as it decelerated, producing a shower …
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. On February 12, 1947, witnesses in the Sikhote-Alin mountains saw a fireball brighter than the sun at 10:38 AM. The iron meteorite fragmented as it decelerated, producing a shower of sharp, angular shrapnel individuals in 23 craters across 2 km². These regmaglypts — thumb-print-like depressions from atmospheric ablation — were literally sculpted by air molecules at 15 km/s.

This specimen originates from Sikhote-Alin Mountains, Primorsky Krai, Russia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Gibeon Iron Meteorite — Etched Slice — Polished Face
short_description: The Widmanstätten pattern — those angular geometric bands revealed by acid etching — took one million years per millimeter to form as the Gibeon parent body cooled in the vacuum of…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. The Widmanstätten pattern — those angular geometric bands revealed by acid etching — took one million years per millimeter to form as the Gibeon parent body cooled in the vacuum of space at approximately one degree per million years. You cannot fake this. It is the slowest signature in the universe. These irons fell across 275×100 km of Namibia, an ancient strewn field from a collision long before Earth had life.

name: Darwin Glass — Tasmania — Etched Section
short_description: Darwin glass formed 816,000 years ago when a meteorite struck western Tasmania, creating a 1.2-kilometer crater. The glass — pale green, pale grey, white, and bubbly — was melted f…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Darwin glass formed 816,000 years ago when a meteorite struck western Tasmania, creating a 1.2-kilometer crater. The glass — pale green, pale grey, white, and bubbly — was melted from local quartzite and dolerite. Unlike moldavite's aerodynamic shaping, Darwin glass shows the chaos of a close-in explosion: twisted, vesicular, sometimes bubbly with trapped gas.

This specimen originates from Darwin Crater, Tasmania, Australia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Carbonaceous Chondrite — CM2 — Polished Face
short_description: Carbonaceous chondrites are the most primitive meteorites — some contain presolar grains older than the solar system itself, including nanodiamonds and silicon carbide formed in ot…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Carbonaceous chondrites are the most primitive meteorites — some contain presolar grains older than the solar system itself, including nanodiamonds and silicon carbide formed in other star systems before our Sun ignited. CM2 chondrites also contain amino acids, complex organics, and water-bearing minerals. The building blocks of life arrived by meteorite: this is no longer a metaphor.

This specimen originates from Northwest Africa, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Imilac Pallasite — Large Slice
short_description: Imilac pallasites from Chile's Atacama Desert show extraordinary olivine crystal size and clarity. The dry desert preserved the metal matrix against weathering while the olivine cr…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Imilac pallasites from Chile's Atacama Desert show extraordinary olivine crystal size and clarity. The dry desert preserved the metal matrix against weathering while the olivine crystals — gem-quality peridot in composition — remained intact. Cut and polished thin sections transmit light through the olivine in warm yellow-green, while the etched metal shows Widmanstätten pattern. It is both geology and jewelry.

This specimen originates from Atacama Desert, Chile, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Pallasite Meteorite — Olivine in Metal — Etched Section
short_description: Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic collision. The result — golden olivine crystals floating in a metallic matrix, backlit like stained glass — is one of the most beautiful materials in the solar system. Brahin pallasites show exceptional olivine clarity.

This specimen originates from Brahin, Gomel Region, Belarus, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Australite Button — Large Slice
short_description: Australites are tektites from the Australasian strewn field — impact glass formed 780,000 years ago, likely from a Southeast Asian impact. The distinctive 'button' shape — a disk w…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Australites are tektites from the Australasian strewn field — impact glass formed 780,000 years ago, likely from a Southeast Asian impact. The distinctive 'button' shape — a disk with a flanged rim — formed as the glass sphere re-entered the atmosphere at an oblique angle, partially melting and being sculpted by atmospheric pressure into this aerodynamic form. They are natural atmospheric re-entry vehicles.

This specimen originates from South Australia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. collector_notes: Store on a padded surface; avoid contact with harder minerals.

name: Moldavite — Czech Tektite — Fragment
short_description: 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravi…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravia. Moldavite is that impact glass — each piece formed in less than a second from vaporized European crust, then shaped by supersonic flight before cooling in free fall. The green color comes from iron.

This specimen originates from Bohemia and Moravia, Czech Republic, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Chondrite Meteorite — L4 Type — Large Slice
short_description: Chondrites are the oldest solid matter in the solar system — 4.56 billion years old, predating Earth by 30–40 million years. Chondrules — spherical beads visible in a fresh cut — w…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Chondrites are the oldest solid matter in the solar system — 4.56 billion years old, predating Earth by 30–40 million years. Chondrules — spherical beads visible in a fresh cut — were flash-melted from nebular dust by poorly understood events in the solar nebula. The 'L' designation means low iron; the '4' means moderate metamorphism. This is literal building material from the protoplanetary disk.

description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Seymchan was originally classified as an iron meteorite when first found in 1967; only later were olivine-bearing sections discovered. The Seymchan parent body was therefore caught in transition from iron meteorite to pallasite — a snapshot of asteroid differentiation in progress. It is one of meteorite science's most interesting classification ambiguities.

This specimen originates from Magadan Oblast, Russia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.
formation: This material formed outside Earth's atmosphere, in the early solar system. Its arrival on Earth was the conclusion of a journey billions of years and hundreds of millions of kilometers in the making — from protoplanetary accretion to atmospheric entry to the ground beneath your feet.
locality_detail: Seymchan Pallasite — Polished Face from Magadan Oblast, Russia. This locality is known for producing specimens of exceptional quality.
collector_notes: Best displayed where natural light can play across the surfaces at different times of day.

name: Australite Button — Etched Section
short_description: Australites are tektites from the Australasian strewn field — impact glass formed 780,000 years ago, likely from a Southeast Asian impact. The distinctive 'button' shape — a disk w…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Australites are tektites from the Australasian strewn field — impact glass formed 780,000 years ago, likely from a Southeast Asian impact. The distinctive 'button' shape — a disk with a flanged rim — formed as the glass sphere re-entered the atmosphere at an oblique angle, partially melting and being sculpted by atmospheric pressure into this aerodynamic form. They are natural atmospheric re-entry vehicles.

This specimen originates from South Australia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.

collector_notes: Store on a padded surface; avoid contact with harder minerals.

name: Moldavite — Czech Tektite — Large Slice
short_description: 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravi…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravia. Moldavite is that impact glass — each piece formed in less than a second from vaporized European crust, then shaped by supersonic flight before cooling in free fall. The green color comes from iron.

This specimen originates from Bohemia and Moravia, Czech Republic, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.

locality_detail: Moldavite — Czech Tektite — Large Slice from Bohemia and Moravia, Czech Republic. This locality is known for producing specimens of exceptional quality.
collector_notes: Best displayed where natural light can play across the surfaces at different times of day.

name: Campo del Cielo Iron Meteorite
short_description: Campo del Cielo — 'Field of the Sky' — fell approximately 4,500 years ago across the Gran Chaco. Indigenous peoples knew the crater field; the first European report came in 1576 fr…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Campo del Cielo — 'Field of the Sky' — fell approximately 4,500 years ago across the Gran Chaco. Indigenous peoples knew the crater field; the first European report came in 1576 from a Spanish governor who noted 'a mass of iron.' The fall produced multiple craters; the combined mass recovered exceeds 100 tonnes, making it one of the world's largest recorded meteorite fields.

This specimen originates from Chaco Province, Argentina, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

collector_notes: Display away from direct sunlight; some minerals fade under prolonged UV exposure.

name: Imilac Pallasite — Polished Face
short_description: Imilac pallasites from Chile's Atacama Desert show extraordinary olivine crystal size and clarity. The dry desert preserved the metal matrix against weathering while the olivine cr…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Imilac pallasites from Chile's Atacama Desert show extraordinary olivine crystal size and clarity. The dry desert preserved the metal matrix against weathering while the olivine crystals — gem-quality peridot in composition — remained intact. Cut and polished thin sections transmit light through the olivine in warm yellow-green, while the etched metal shows Widmanstätten pattern. It is both geology and jewelry.

This specimen originates from Atacama Desert, Chile, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Libyan Desert Glass — Small Individual
short_description: Libyan desert glass — found in a 6,500 km² strewn field — is the purest natural silica glass on Earth. Its formation 29 million years ago involved temperatures exceeding 1600°C, su…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. Libyan desert glass — found in a 6,500 km² strewn field — is the purest natural silica glass on Earth. Its formation 29 million years ago involved temperatures exceeding 1600°C, suggesting an airburst or impact-generated thermal event so intense it flash-melted the Saharan sand. A carved Libyan desert glass scarab was found in Tutankhamun's pectoral — ancient Egyptians recognized it as extraordinary.

This specimen originates from Great Sand Sea, Libya/Egypt, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Pallasite Meteorite — Olivine in Metal — Small Individual
short_description: Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic collision. The result — golden olivine crystals floating in a metallic matrix, backlit like stained glass — is one of the most beautiful materials in the solar system. Brahin pallasites show exceptional olivine clarity.

This specimen originates from Brahin, Gomel Region, Belarus, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Sikhote-Alin Individual — Shrapnel — Large Slice
short_description: On February 12, 1947, witnesses in the Sikhote-Alin mountains saw a fireball brighter than the sun at 10:38 AM. The iron meteorite fragmented as it decelerated, producing a shower …
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. On February 12, 1947, witnesses in the Sikhote-Alin mountains saw a fireball brighter than the sun at 10:38 AM. The iron meteorite fragmented as it decelerated, producing a shower of sharp, angular shrapnel individuals in 23 craters across 2 km². These regmaglypts — thumb-print-like depressions from atmospheric ablation — were literally sculpted by air molecules at 15 km/s.

This specimen originates from Sikhote-Alin Mountains, Primorsky Krai, Russia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.
formation: This material formed outside Earth's atmosphere, in the early solar system. Its arrival on Earth was the conclusion of a journey billions of years and hundreds of millions of kilometers in the making — from protoplanetary accretion to atmospheric entry to the ground beneath your feet.
locality_detail: Sikhote-Alin Individual — Shrapnel — Large Slice from Sikhote-Alin Mountains, Primorsky Krai, Russia. This locality is known for producing specimens of exceptional quality.
collector_notes: The luster is maximized by low-angle raking light — try a single spotlight.

name: Lunar Meteorite — Mare Basalt — Large Slice
short_description: These were once rocks on the lunar maria — the dark volcanic plains visible with the naked eye from Earth. An asteroid impact on the Moon ejected this material with enough velocity…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. These were once rocks on the lunar maria — the dark volcanic plains visible with the naked eye from Earth. An asteroid impact on the Moon ejected this material with enough velocity to escape lunar gravity. It traveled through space for millions of years before entering Earth's atmosphere. You can hold the Moon.

This specimen originates from Northwest Africa (NWA 032 type), one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Martian Meteorite — Shergottite — Polished Face
short_description: The ultimate provenance story: this rock formed on Mars in a volcanic eruption approximately 180 million years ago. A massive impact blasted it from the Martian surface with enough…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The ultimate provenance story: this rock formed on Mars in a volcanic eruption approximately 180 million years ago. A massive impact blasted it from the Martian surface with enough force to achieve escape velocity. It traveled through interplanetary space before landing on Earth. Martian origin is verified by trapped gas matching Viking lander measurements of the Martian atmosphere.

This specimen originates from Northwest Africa (NWA 2975 type), one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Australite Button — Small Individual

description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Australites are tektites from the Australasian strewn field — impact glass formed 780,000 years ago, likely from a Southeast Asian impact. The distinctive 'button' shape — a disk with a flanged rim — formed as the glass sphere re-entered the atmosphere at an oblique angle, partially melting and being sculpted by atmospheric pressure into this aerodynamic form. They are natural atmospheric re-entry vehicles.

name: Moldavite — Czech Tektite — Etched Section
short_description: 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravi…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravia. Moldavite is that impact glass — each piece formed in less than a second from vaporized European crust, then shaped by supersonic flight before cooling in free fall. The green color comes from iron.

This specimen originates from Bohemia and Moravia, Czech Republic, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Sikhote-Alin Individual — Shrapnel — Small Individual
short_description: On February 12, 1947, witnesses in the Sikhote-Alin mountains saw a fireball brighter than the sun at 10:38 AM. The iron meteorite fragmented as it decelerated, producing a shower …
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. On February 12, 1947, witnesses in the Sikhote-Alin mountains saw a fireball brighter than the sun at 10:38 AM. The iron meteorite fragmented as it decelerated, producing a shower of sharp, angular shrapnel individuals in 23 craters across 2 km². These regmaglypts — thumb-print-like depressions from atmospheric ablation — were literally sculpted by air molecules at 15 km/s.

This specimen originates from Sikhote-Alin Mountains, Primorsky Krai, Russia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Carbonaceous chondrites are the most primitive meteorites — some contain presolar grains older than the solar system itself, including nanodiamonds and silicon carbide formed in other star systems before our Sun ignited. CM2 chondrites also contain amino acids, complex organics, and water-bearing minerals. The building blocks of life arrived by meteorite: this is no longer a metaphor.

name: Campo del Cielo Iron Meteorite — Oriented Individual
short_description: Campo del Cielo — 'Field of the Sky' — fell approximately 4,500 years ago across the Gran Chaco. Indigenous peoples knew the crater field; the first European report came in 1576 fr…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Campo del Cielo — 'Field of the Sky' — fell approximately 4,500 years ago across the Gran Chaco. Indigenous peoples knew the crater field; the first European report came in 1576 from a Spanish governor who noted 'a mass of iron.' The fall produced multiple craters; the combined mass recovered exceeds 100 tonnes, making it one of the world's largest recorded meteorite fields.

This specimen originates from Chaco Province, Argentina, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Darwin Glass — Tasmania
short_description: Darwin glass formed 816,000 years ago when a meteorite struck western Tasmania, creating a 1.2-kilometer crater. The glass — pale green, pale grey, white, and bubbly — was melted f…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Darwin glass formed 816,000 years ago when a meteorite struck western Tasmania, creating a 1.2-kilometer crater. The glass — pale green, pale grey, white, and bubbly — was melted from local quartzite and dolerite. Unlike moldavite's aerodynamic shaping, Darwin glass shows the chaos of a close-in explosion: twisted, vesicular, sometimes bubbly with trapped gas.

This specimen originates from Darwin Crater, Tasmania, Australia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Martian Meteorite — Shergottite
short_description: The ultimate provenance story: this rock formed on Mars in a volcanic eruption approximately 180 million years ago. A massive impact blasted it from the Martian surface with enough…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. The ultimate provenance story: this rock formed on Mars in a volcanic eruption approximately 180 million years ago. A massive impact blasted it from the Martian surface with enough force to achieve escape velocity. It traveled through interplanetary space before landing on Earth. Martian origin is verified by trapped gas matching Viking lander measurements of the Martian atmosphere.

This specimen originates from Northwest Africa (NWA 2975 type), one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

collector_notes: Best displayed where natural light can play across the surfaces at different times of day.

name: Chondrite Meteorite — L4 Type — Polished Face
short_description: Chondrites are the oldest solid matter in the solar system — 4.56 billion years old, predating Earth by 30–40 million years. Chondrules — spherical beads visible in a fresh cut — w…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Chondrites are the oldest solid matter in the solar system — 4.56 billion years old, predating Earth by 30–40 million years. Chondrules — spherical beads visible in a fresh cut — were flash-melted from nebular dust by poorly understood events in the solar nebula. The 'L' designation means low iron; the '4' means moderate metamorphism. This is literal building material from the protoplanetary disk.

This specimen originates from Northwest Africa (NWA), one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Iron Meteorite — Toluca — Etched Section
short_description: The Toluca iron was first reported in 1776 and became one of the most-distributed meteorites in museums worldwide. Its Widmanstätten pattern is particularly coarse — wide bands ind…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. The Toluca iron was first reported in 1776 and became one of the most-distributed meteorites in museums worldwide. Its Widmanstätten pattern is particularly coarse — wide bands indicating slow cooling from a large parent body. The fall likely occurred before recorded history; Aztec artifacts of meteoric iron have been found in the region.

This specimen originates from Toluca Valley, Mexico, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Pallasite Meteorite — Olivine in Metal — Large Slice
short_description: Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Pallasites form at the core-mantle boundary of differentiated asteroids: iron-nickel metal from the core mixed with olivine crystals from the mantle at the moment of a catastrophic collision. The result — golden olivine crystals floating in a metallic matrix, backlit like stained glass — is one of the most beautiful materials in the solar system. Brahin pallasites show exceptional olivine clarity.

name: Moldavite — Czech Tektite — Small Individual
short_description: 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravi…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. 15 million years ago, a 1.5-kilometer asteroid struck what is now Bavaria, generating a fireball that melted local surface silica and splashed liquid rock across Bohemia and Moravia. Moldavite is that impact glass — each piece formed in less than a second from vaporized European crust, then shaped by supersonic flight before cooling in free fall. The green color comes from iron.

name: Iron Meteorite — Toluca — Small Individual
short_description: The Toluca iron was first reported in 1776 and became one of the most-distributed meteorites in museums worldwide. Its Widmanstätten pattern is particularly coarse — wide bands ind…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. The Toluca iron was first reported in 1776 and became one of the most-distributed meteorites in museums worldwide. Its Widmanstätten pattern is particularly coarse — wide bands indicating slow cooling from a large parent body. The fall likely occurred before recorded history; Aztec artifacts of meteoric iron have been found in the region.

name: Gibeon Iron Meteorite — Etched Slice — Etched Section
short_description: The Widmanstätten pattern — those angular geometric bands revealed by acid etching — took one million years per millimeter to form as the Gibeon parent body cooled in the vacuum of…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The Widmanstätten pattern — those angular geometric bands revealed by acid etching — took one million years per millimeter to form as the Gibeon parent body cooled in the vacuum of space at approximately one degree per million years. You cannot fake this. It is the slowest signature in the universe. These irons fell across 275×100 km of Namibia, an ancient strewn field from a collision long before Earth had life.

name: Martian Meteorite — Shergottite — Small Individual
short_description: The ultimate provenance story: this rock formed on Mars in a volcanic eruption approximately 180 million years ago. A massive impact blasted it from the Martian surface with enough…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. The ultimate provenance story: this rock formed on Mars in a volcanic eruption approximately 180 million years ago. A massive impact blasted it from the Martian surface with enough force to achieve escape velocity. It traveled through interplanetary space before landing on Earth. Martian origin is verified by trapped gas matching Viking lander measurements of the Martian atmosphere.

name: Libyan Desert Glass — Etched Section
short_description: Libyan desert glass — found in a 6,500 km² strewn field — is the purest natural silica glass on Earth. Its formation 29 million years ago involved temperatures exceeding 1600°C, su…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Libyan desert glass — found in a 6,500 km² strewn field — is the purest natural silica glass on Earth. Its formation 29 million years ago involved temperatures exceeding 1600°C, suggesting an airburst or impact-generated thermal event so intense it flash-melted the Saharan sand. A carved Libyan desert glass scarab was found in Tutankhamun's pectoral — ancient Egyptians recognized it as extraordinary.

name: Chondrite Meteorite — L4 Type — Etched Section
short_description: Chondrites are the oldest solid matter in the solar system — 4.56 billion years old, predating Earth by 30–40 million years. Chondrules — spherical beads visible in a fresh cut — w…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Chondrites are the oldest solid matter in the solar system — 4.56 billion years old, predating Earth by 30–40 million years. Chondrules — spherical beads visible in a fresh cut — were flash-melted from nebular dust by poorly understood events in the solar nebula. The 'L' designation means low iron; the '4' means moderate metamorphism. This is literal building material from the protoplanetary disk.

name: Fulgurite — Desert Glass Tube — Display Grade
short_description: When lightning strikes sand, the 30,000°C channel fuses it into a hollow tube of silica glass in less than a second. Fulgurites record the exact path of a lightning bolt's passage:…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. When lightning strikes sand, the 30,000°C channel fuses it into a hollow tube of silica glass in less than a second. Fulgurites record the exact path of a lightning bolt's passage: branching, tubular, with a glassy interior and sandy exterior. The longest ever found exceeded 4.9 meters, tracing a bolt that struck once and left a monument of glass.

name: Pele's Tears — Hawaiian
short_description: Named for the Hawaiian volcano goddess, Pele's tears are small spheroids of basaltic glass formed when lava fountains eject droplets that solidify in flight. Often connected by hai…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Named for the Hawaiian volcano goddess, Pele's tears are small spheroids of basaltic glass formed when lava fountains eject droplets that solidify in flight. Often connected by hair-thin threads of glass (Pele's hair), each teardrop captures a moment of volcanic energy in a perfect miniature form. The black glass is essentially identical to obsidian, formed in seconds rather than years.

name: Chalk with Flint Nodule — Display Grade
short_description: Flint nodules grew within Cretaceous chalk as silica dissolved from sponge spicules and radiolarians was reprecipitated around organic nuclei. The result is a microcrystalline sili…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Flint nodules grew within Cretaceous chalk as silica dissolved from sponge spicules and radiolarians was reprecipitated around organic nuclei. The result is a microcrystalline silica nodule with a smooth, cream to grey exterior and a waxy, conchoidal interior. Paleolithic humans depended on flint technology for 2 million years — its predictable fracture pattern is the geological foundation of human civilization.

name: Obsidian — Mahogany Flow
locality: Glass Buttes, Lake County, Oregon, USA
short_description: Obsidian is volcanic glass — lava that cooled so fast that atoms had no time to arrange into crystals. Mahogany obsidian from Oregon's Glass Buttes shows concentric bands of black …
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Obsidian is volcanic glass — lava that cooled so fast that atoms had no time to arrange into crystals. Mahogany obsidian from Oregon's Glass Buttes shows concentric bands of black and mahogany-brown (iron oxide) laid down as separate lava flows mingled. Indigenous peoples made obsidian blades sharp enough to outcut surgical steel by single-molecule-edge fracturing — a technology not matched by metal until the 20th century.

name: Septarian Nodule — Polished Half — Polished Face
short_description: Septarian nodules form in marine muds: organic-rich sediment hardens into an oval nodule, then shrinks as it dries, cracking into a cage-like pattern ('septa') that fills with calc…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Septarian nodules form in marine muds: organic-rich sediment hardens into an oval nodule, then shrinks as it dries, cracking into a cage-like pattern ('septa') that fills with calcite, aragonite, or barite crystals. Polished in half, the resulting pattern looks like stained glass in earth tones — a natural mosaic that took millions of years to complete.

name: Pumice from Santorini — Display Grade
short_description: The Minoan eruption of Santorini (~1600 BCE) was one of the largest volcanic events in human history — it produced enough pumice to bury its contemporaries. Pumice is volcanic glas…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. The Minoan eruption of Santorini (~1600 BCE) was one of the largest volcanic events in human history — it produced enough pumice to bury its contemporaries. Pumice is volcanic glass so thoroughly vesiculated that it floats on water. Santorini pumice has been found at the bottoms of the Nile and on the shores of Israel — tsunami evidence from the eruption that may have inspired the Atlantis legend.

name: Garnet Schist — Polished Face
short_description: In garnet schist, the pressure and temperature of mountain building are made visible: almandine garnet porphyroblasts grew during regional metamorphism, their chemistry recording t…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. In garnet schist, the pressure and temperature of mountain building are made visible: almandine garnet porphyroblasts grew during regional metamorphism, their chemistry recording the conditions precisely enough that geologists can calculate the depth and temperature of burial. The Austrian Alps expose some of Europe's deepest metamorphic terrains — these specimens were formed at the roots of ancient mountains.

name: Banded Iron Formation
short_description: These are the oldest beautiful objects on Earth. Banded iron formations (BIFs) formed between 1.8–2.5 billion years ago, when cyanobacteria — the first photosynthetic organisms — b…
description:
Consider what had to happen for this specimen to exist. These are the oldest beautiful objects on Earth. Banded iron formations (BIFs) formed between 1.8–2.5 billion years ago, when cyanobacteria — the first photosynthetic organisms — began producing oxygen that oxidized dissolved iron from seawater. The alternating iron oxide and chert bands record the rhythmic oxygenation of Earth's early oceans. This rock helped make Earth's atmosphere breathable.

name: Volcanic Bomb — Breadcrust — Display Grade
short_description: Breadcrust bombs are blobs of lava ejected from volcanoes that crack on the surface during flight — the interior continues expanding from dissolved gas while the outer skin cools a…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Breadcrust bombs are blobs of lava ejected from volcanoes that crack on the surface during flight — the interior continues expanding from dissolved gas while the outer skin cools and solidifies, producing the distinctive cracked-crust pattern. Stromboli has been erupting continuously for 2,000+ years; these bombs were formed in eruptions witnessed by human observers.

name: Oolitic Limestone — Display Grade
short_description: Oolitic limestone is made of 'ooids' — tiny calcite spheres formed in shallow, warm, agitated seawater (like the Bahamas today) where calcium carbonate accretes concentrically arou…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. Oolitic limestone is made of 'ooids' — tiny calcite spheres formed in shallow, warm, agitated seawater (like the Bahamas today) where calcium carbonate accretes concentrically around a grain nucleus. Cutting and polishing reveals perfect spheres, each 0.25–2 mm, packed in a calcite mosaic. The Jurassic seas that covered England were indistinguishable from the modern tropics.

short_description: In garnet schist, the pressure and temperature of mountain building are made visible: almandine garnet porphyroblasts grew during regional metamorphism, their chemistry recording t…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. In garnet schist, the pressure and temperature of mountain building are made visible: almandine garnet porphyroblasts grew during regional metamorphism, their chemistry recording the conditions precisely enough that geologists can calculate the depth and temperature of burial. The Austrian Alps expose some of Europe's deepest metamorphic terrains — these specimens were formed at the roots of ancient mountains.

This specimen originates from Tauern Window, Austria, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.

The specific combination of chemistry, temperature, pressure, and time at this locality produced material of exceptional quality that makes it a valued addition to any serious collection.

name: Conglomerate — Tillite — Large Specimen
short_description: The Gowganda tillite (2.3 billion years old) is evidence of Earth's first global glaciation — the 'Huronian' ice age that followed the Great Oxidation Event. Glaciers transported b…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. The Gowganda tillite (2.3 billion years old) is evidence of Earth's first global glaciation — the 'Huronian' ice age that followed the Great Oxidation Event. Glaciers transported boulders of mixed composition and deposited them in an unsorted matrix. This rock records a time when ice sheets reached the equator, long before complex life evolved.

This specimen originates from Gowganda Formation, Ontario, Canada, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Amethyst Cathedral Geode — Half — Display Grade
short_description: These cathedral-shaped geodes form in basalt vesicles — gas bubbles from ancient lava flows — over millions of years as silica-rich groundwater precipitates quartz. The purple amet…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. These cathedral-shaped geodes form in basalt vesicles — gas bubbles from ancient lava flows — over millions of years as silica-rich groundwater precipitates quartz. The purple amethyst color forms in the last stage, when trace iron is present. A geode half shows the full geological biography: the outer chalcedony bands recording early mineral deposition, the amethyst crystal forest pointing inward.

This specimen originates from Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Suevite — Impact Breccia — Large Specimen
short_description: Suevite is impact breccia containing glass bombs and shocked minerals — the concrete of asteroid impact craters. Nördlingen Ries, formed 14.5 million years ago by a 1.5-km asteroid…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Suevite is impact breccia containing glass bombs and shocked minerals — the concrete of asteroid impact craters. Nördlingen Ries, formed 14.5 million years ago by a 1.5-km asteroid, produced suevite so abundant that the medieval city of Nördlingen was built almost entirely from it. The city's walls contain millions of microscopic diamonds formed by the impact pressure.

This specimen originates from Nördlingen Ries Crater, Bavaria, Germany, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Radiolarite — Polished Face
locality: Chert Melange, Marin County, California, USA
short_description: Radiolarites are siliceous sedimentary rocks formed from the accumulated silica skeletons of radiolarians — single-celled marine organisms with intricate geometric glass skeletons …
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Radiolarites are siliceous sedimentary rocks formed from the accumulated silica skeletons of radiolarians — single-celled marine organisms with intricate geometric glass skeletons — deposited on the deep ocean floor over millions of years. California's Marin Headlands radiolarites, now exposed as the Franciscan Complex, were formed in the middle of the Pacific and scraped off as the oceanic plate subducted under North America.

This specimen originates from Chert Melange, Marin County, California, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Serpentinite — Display Grade
short_description: Serpentinite is oceanic mantle rock that has reacted with seawater — the green color comes from serpentine group minerals replacing olivine and pyroxene. The Troodos ophiolite in C…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Serpentinite is oceanic mantle rock that has reacted with seawater — the green color comes from serpentine group minerals replacing olivine and pyroxene. The Troodos ophiolite in Cyprus is a slice of ancient ocean floor uplifted by tectonic collision: these rocks formed at a spreading ridge, were hydrated on the seafloor, then thrust onto a continent. Cyprus means 'copper island' — the ancient mines worked the copper ore bodies in this very ophiolite.

This specimen originates from Troodos Ophiolite, Cyprus, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Desert Varnish Sandstone — Specimen Grade
short_description: Desert varnish — the dark rind coating canyon walls and boulders throughout arid regions — is deposited by microorganisms over thousands of years. Bacteria concentrate manganese fr…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Desert varnish — the dark rind coating canyon walls and boulders throughout arid regions — is deposited by microorganisms over thousands of years. Bacteria concentrate manganese from windblown dust and bind it to rock surfaces in a manganese-iron oxide layer sometimes only micrometers thick. Ancient petroglyphs are created by scratching through varnish to expose lighter rock — the varnish dates the minimum age of the art.

This specimen originates from Colorado Plateau, Utah, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Volcanic Bomb — Breadcrust — Large Specimen
short_description: Breadcrust bombs are blobs of lava ejected from volcanoes that crack on the surface during flight — the interior continues expanding from dissolved gas while the outer skin cools a…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Breadcrust bombs are blobs of lava ejected from volcanoes that crack on the surface during flight — the interior continues expanding from dissolved gas while the outer skin cools and solidifies, producing the distinctive cracked-crust pattern. Stromboli has been erupting continuously for 2,000+ years; these bombs were formed in eruptions witnessed by human observers.

This specimen originates from Stromboli, Sicily, Italy, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Celestite Geode — Whole
short_description: Ohio's celestite geodes formed in Devonian limestone beds 380 million years ago, when strontium-bearing solutions replaced calcite in pre-existing voids. The result is geodes lined…
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Ohio's celestite geodes formed in Devonian limestone beds 380 million years ago, when strontium-bearing solutions replaced calcite in pre-existing voids. The result is geodes lined with pale blue to clear orthorhombic crystals — the largest geode of any kind in North America (Crystal Cave on Put-in-Bay Island) is celestite. Crack one open and find a sky.

name: Banded Iron Formation — Polished Face
short_description: These are the oldest beautiful objects on Earth. Banded iron formations (BIFs) formed between 1.8–2.5 billion years ago, when cyanobacteria — the first photosynthetic organisms — b…
description:
What looks like stone is, on closer inspection, frozen time. These are the oldest beautiful objects on Earth. Banded iron formations (BIFs) formed between 1.8–2.5 billion years ago, when cyanobacteria — the first photosynthetic organisms — began producing oxygen that oxidized dissolved iron from seawater. The alternating iron oxide and chert bands record the rhythmic oxygenation of Earth's early oceans. This rock helped make Earth's atmosphere breathable.

This specimen originates from Hamersley Basin, Western Australia, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Eclogite — Specimen Grade
short_description: Eclogite is crustal rock metamorphosed under extreme pressure — 30–40 km deep — as tectonic plates subduct. The diagnostic assemblage of red pyrope garnet and bright green omphacit…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Eclogite is crustal rock metamorphosed under extreme pressure — 30–40 km deep — as tectonic plates subduct. The diagnostic assemblage of red pyrope garnet and bright green omphacite pyroxene creates a visually striking rock that no surface process can produce. Norwegian eclogites were once part of the continental crust before being dragged down and brought back up by orogenesis.

This specimen originates from Bergen Arcs, Norway, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

name: Septarian Nodule — Polished Half — Large Specimen
short_description: Septarian nodules form in marine muds: organic-rich sediment hardens into an oval nodule, then shrinks as it dries, cracking into a cage-like pattern ('septa') that fills with calc…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Septarian nodules form in marine muds: organic-rich sediment hardens into an oval nodule, then shrinks as it dries, cracking into a cage-like pattern ('septa') that fills with calcite, aragonite, or barite crystals. Polished in half, the resulting pattern looks like stained glass in earth tones — a natural mosaic that took millions of years to complete.

This specimen originates from Bingham, Utah, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Shatter Cone — Specimen Grade
short_description: Shatter cones are the diagnostic proof of hypervelocity impact: concentric, striated conical fractures produced only by shock pressures exceeding 2–30 GPa. No other geological proc…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Shatter cones are the diagnostic proof of hypervelocity impact: concentric, striated conical fractures produced only by shock pressures exceeding 2–30 GPa. No other geological process produces them. Sudbury's shatter cones formed 1.85 billion years ago in the second-largest confirmed impact on Earth. The Sudbury crater (now eroded to a basin) is 130 km across.

name: Fulgurite — Desert Glass Tube — Polished Face
short_description: When lightning strikes sand, the 30,000°C channel fuses it into a hollow tube of silica glass in less than a second. Fulgurites record the exact path of a lightning bolt's passage:…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. When lightning strikes sand, the 30,000°C channel fuses it into a hollow tube of silica glass in less than a second. Fulgurites record the exact path of a lightning bolt's passage: branching, tubular, with a glassy interior and sandy exterior. The longest ever found exceeded 4.9 meters, tracing a bolt that struck once and left a monument of glass.

name: Septarian Nodule — Polished Half — Specimen Grade
short_description: Septarian nodules form in marine muds: organic-rich sediment hardens into an oval nodule, then shrinks as it dries, cracking into a cage-like pattern ('septa') that fills with calc…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Septarian nodules form in marine muds: organic-rich sediment hardens into an oval nodule, then shrinks as it dries, cracking into a cage-like pattern ('septa') that fills with calcite, aragonite, or barite crystals. Polished in half, the resulting pattern looks like stained glass in earth tones — a natural mosaic that took millions of years to complete.

description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Eclogite is crustal rock metamorphosed under extreme pressure — 30–40 km deep — as tectonic plates subduct. The diagnostic assemblage of red pyrope garnet and bright green omphacite pyroxene creates a visually striking rock that no surface process can produce. Norwegian eclogites were once part of the continental crust before being dragged down and brought back up by orogenesis.

name: Chalk with Flint Nodule
short_description: Flint nodules grew within Cretaceous chalk as silica dissolved from sponge spicules and radiolarians was reprecipitated around organic nuclei. The result is a microcrystalline sili…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Flint nodules grew within Cretaceous chalk as silica dissolved from sponge spicules and radiolarians was reprecipitated around organic nuclei. The result is a microcrystalline silica nodule with a smooth, cream to grey exterior and a waxy, conchoidal interior. Paleolithic humans depended on flint technology for 2 million years — its predictable fracture pattern is the geological foundation of human civilization.

name: Radiolarite
locality: Chert Melange, Marin County, California, USA
short_description: Radiolarites are siliceous sedimentary rocks formed from the accumulated silica skeletons of radiolarians — single-celled marine organisms with intricate geometric glass skeletons …
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Radiolarites are siliceous sedimentary rocks formed from the accumulated silica skeletons of radiolarians — single-celled marine organisms with intricate geometric glass skeletons — deposited on the deep ocean floor over millions of years. California's Marin Headlands radiolarites, now exposed as the Franciscan Complex, were formed in the middle of the Pacific and scraped off as the oceanic plate subducted under North America.

name: Amethyst Cathedral Geode — Half
short_description: These cathedral-shaped geodes form in basalt vesicles — gas bubbles from ancient lava flows — over millions of years as silica-rich groundwater precipitates quartz. The purple amet…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. These cathedral-shaped geodes form in basalt vesicles — gas bubbles from ancient lava flows — over millions of years as silica-rich groundwater precipitates quartz. The purple amethyst color forms in the last stage, when trace iron is present. A geode half shows the full geological biography: the outer chalcedony bands recording early mineral deposition, the amethyst crystal forest pointing inward.

name: Oolitic Limestone — Specimen Grade
short_description: Oolitic limestone is made of 'ooids' — tiny calcite spheres formed in shallow, warm, agitated seawater (like the Bahamas today) where calcium carbonate accretes concentrically arou…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Oolitic limestone is made of 'ooids' — tiny calcite spheres formed in shallow, warm, agitated seawater (like the Bahamas today) where calcium carbonate accretes concentrically around a grain nucleus. Cutting and polishing reveals perfect spheres, each 0.25–2 mm, packed in a calcite mosaic. The Jurassic seas that covered England were indistinguishable from the modern tropics.

name: Desert Varnish Sandstone — Large Specimen
short_description: Desert varnish — the dark rind coating canyon walls and boulders throughout arid regions — is deposited by microorganisms over thousands of years. Bacteria concentrate manganese fr…
description:
The universe has 13.8 billion years of history. Some of it fits in your hand. Desert varnish — the dark rind coating canyon walls and boulders throughout arid regions — is deposited by microorganisms over thousands of years. Bacteria concentrate manganese from windblown dust and bind it to rock surfaces in a manganese-iron oxide layer sometimes only micrometers thick. Ancient petroglyphs are created by scratching through varnish to expose lighter rock — the varnish dates the minimum age of the art.

name: Obsidian — Mahogany Flow — Large Specimen
locality: Glass Buttes, Lake County, Oregon, USA
short_description: Obsidian is volcanic glass — lava that cooled so fast that atoms had no time to arrange into crystals. Mahogany obsidian from Oregon's Glass Buttes shows concentric bands of black …
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Obsidian is volcanic glass — lava that cooled so fast that atoms had no time to arrange into crystals. Mahogany obsidian from Oregon's Glass Buttes shows concentric bands of black and mahogany-brown (iron oxide) laid down as separate lava flows mingled. Indigenous peoples made obsidian blades sharp enough to outcut surgical steel by single-molecule-edge fracturing — a technology not matched by metal until the 20th century.

short_description: Septarian nodules form in marine muds: organic-rich sediment hardens into an oval nodule, then shrinks as it dries, cracking into a cage-like pattern ('septa') that fills with calc…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Septarian nodules form in marine muds: organic-rich sediment hardens into an oval nodule, then shrinks as it dries, cracking into a cage-like pattern ('septa') that fills with calcite, aragonite, or barite crystals. Polished in half, the resulting pattern looks like stained glass in earth tones — a natural mosaic that took millions of years to complete.

name: Banded Iron Formation — Field Specimen
short_description: These are the oldest beautiful objects on Earth. Banded iron formations (BIFs) formed between 1.8–2.5 billion years ago, when cyanobacteria — the first photosynthetic organisms — b…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. These are the oldest beautiful objects on Earth. Banded iron formations (BIFs) formed between 1.8–2.5 billion years ago, when cyanobacteria — the first photosynthetic organisms — began producing oxygen that oxidized dissolved iron from seawater. The alternating iron oxide and chert bands record the rhythmic oxygenation of Earth's early oceans. This rock helped make Earth's atmosphere breathable.

name: Celestite Geode — Whole — Display Grade
short_description: Ohio's celestite geodes formed in Devonian limestone beds 380 million years ago, when strontium-bearing solutions replaced calcite in pre-existing voids. The result is geodes lined…
description:
Every mineral is a story of chemistry, pressure, and patience. Ohio's celestite geodes formed in Devonian limestone beds 380 million years ago, when strontium-bearing solutions replaced calcite in pre-existing voids. The result is geodes lined with pale blue to clear orthorhombic crystals — the largest geode of any kind in North America (Crystal Cave on Put-in-Bay Island) is celestite. Crack one open and find a sky.

Flint nodules grew within Cretaceous chalk as silica dissolved from sponge spicules and radiolarians was reprecipitated around organic nuclei. The result is a microcrystalline silica nodule with a smooth, cream to grey exterior and a waxy, conchoidal interior. Paleolithic humans depended on flint technology for 2 million years — its predictable fracture pattern is the geological foundation of human civilization.

This specimen originates from Cretaceous chalk, Sussex, England, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.

Every specimen is unique. Photographs approximate the visual experience, but the real thing — its weight, its luster under a raking light, the way it catches the corner of your eye — can only be experienced directly.
formation: This specimen formed through geological processes operating over timescales that dwarf human history. The specific combination of chemistry, temperature, pressure, and time at this locality produced material of exceptional quality that makes it a valued addition to any serious collection.
locality_detail: Chalk with Flint Nodule — Specimen Grade from Cretaceous chalk, Sussex, England. This locality is known for producing specimens of exceptional quality.
collector_notes: Handle with care; crystal terminations are irreplaceable once damaged.

name: Eclogite — Display Grade
short_description: Eclogite is crustal rock metamorphosed under extreme pressure — 30–40 km deep — as tectonic plates subduct. The diagnostic assemblage of red pyrope garnet and bright green omphacit…
description:
There are objects in this world that carry the weight of unimaginable time. Eclogite is crustal rock metamorphosed under extreme pressure — 30–40 km deep — as tectonic plates subduct. The diagnostic assemblage of red pyrope garnet and bright green omphacite pyroxene creates a visually striking rock that no surface process can produce. Norwegian eclogites were once part of the continental crust before being dragged down and brought back up by orogenesis.

This specimen originates from Bergen Arcs, Norway, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Radiolarite — Display Grade
locality: Chert Melange, Marin County, California, USA
short_description: Radiolarites are siliceous sedimentary rocks formed from the accumulated silica skeletons of radiolarians — single-celled marine organisms with intricate geometric glass skeletons …
description:
Collectors say that minerals are the universe's autobiography, written in atoms. Radiolarites are siliceous sedimentary rocks formed from the accumulated silica skeletons of radiolarians — single-celled marine organisms with intricate geometric glass skeletons — deposited on the deep ocean floor over millions of years. California's Marin Headlands radiolarites, now exposed as the Franciscan Complex, were formed in the middle of the Pacific and scraped off as the oceanic plate subducted under North America.

name: Pele's Tears — Hawaiian — Display Grade
short_description: Named for the Hawaiian volcano goddess, Pele's tears are small spheroids of basaltic glass formed when lava fountains eject droplets that solidify in flight. Often connected by hai…
description:
The rarest things on Earth were formed by the most ordinary processes, repeated for eons. Named for the Hawaiian volcano goddess, Pele's tears are small spheroids of basaltic glass formed when lava fountains eject droplets that solidify in flight. Often connected by hair-thin threads of glass (Pele's hair), each teardrop captures a moment of volcanic energy in a perfect miniature form. The black glass is essentially identical to obsidian, formed in seconds rather than years.

name: Obsidian — Mahogany Flow — Display Grade
locality: Glass Buttes, Lake County, Oregon, USA
short_description: Obsidian is volcanic glass — lava that cooled so fast that atoms had no time to arrange into crystals. Mahogany obsidian from Oregon's Glass Buttes shows concentric bands of black …
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Obsidian is volcanic glass — lava that cooled so fast that atoms had no time to arrange into crystals. Mahogany obsidian from Oregon's Glass Buttes shows concentric bands of black and mahogany-brown (iron oxide) laid down as separate lava flows mingled. Indigenous peoples made obsidian blades sharp enough to outcut surgical steel by single-molecule-edge fracturing — a technology not matched by metal until the 20th century.

This specimen originates from Glass Buttes, Lake County, Oregon, USA, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. name: Amethyst Cathedral Geode — Half — Field Specimen
short_description: These cathedral-shaped geodes form in basalt vesicles — gas bubbles from ancient lava flows — over millions of years as silica-rich groundwater precipitates quartz. The purple amet…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. These cathedral-shaped geodes form in basalt vesicles — gas bubbles from ancient lava flows — over millions of years as silica-rich groundwater precipitates quartz. The purple amethyst color forms in the last stage, when trace iron is present. A geode half shows the full geological biography: the outer chalcedony bands recording early mineral deposition, the amethyst crystal forest pointing inward.

name: Suevite — Impact Breccia
short_description: Suevite is impact breccia containing glass bombs and shocked minerals — the concrete of asteroid impact craters. Nördlingen Ries, formed 14.5 million years ago by a 1.5-km asteroid…
description:
Science and beauty are rarely in conflict. Here they converge. Suevite is impact breccia containing glass bombs and shocked minerals — the concrete of asteroid impact craters. Nördlingen Ries, formed 14.5 million years ago by a 1.5-km asteroid, produced suevite so abundant that the medieval city of Nördlingen was built almost entirely from it. The city's walls contain millions of microscopic diamonds formed by the impact pressure.

This specimen originates from Nördlingen Ries Crater, Bavaria, Germany, one of the world's most significant localities for this type of material. Collectors and scientists have drawn from this region for generations, and for good reason: the combination of geological conditions here produces specimens of exceptional quality and clarity.
